[+] Building 89.7s (68/68) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile.jobpost-analytics 0.1s => [internal] load .dockerignore 0.1s => [internal] importing cache manifest from anihpj.azurecr.io/buildcache/ml-models 3.2s => [linux/amd64 ml-base 1/6] FROM python:3.11-slim 0.0s => [linux/arm64 ml-base 1/6] FROM python:3.11-slim 0.0s => CACHED [linux/amd64 ml-base 2/6] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y gcc 0.0s => CACHED [linux/arm64 ml-base 2/6] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y gcc 0.0s => [linux/amd64 ml-deps 1/4] COPY requirements-ml.txt . 0.1s => [linux/arm64 ml-deps 1/4] COPY requirements-ml.txt . 0.1s => [linux/amd64 ml-deps 2/4] RUN pip install scikit-learn==1.3.0 25.3s => [linux/arm64 ml-deps 2/4] RUN pip install scikit-learn==1.3.0 28.7s => [linux/amd64 ml-deps 3/4] RUN pip install tensorflow==2.13.0 32.1s => [linux/arm64 ml-deps 3/4] RUN pip install tensorflow==2.13.0 35.4s => [linux/amd64 production 12/12] CMD ["python", "manage.py", "run_analytics"] 0.1s => [linux/arm64 production 12/12] CMD ["python", "manage.py", "run_analytics"] 0.1s => exporting to image 8.7s => => pushing layers 15.3s => exporting cache 12.1s Analytics build completed with ML optimizations (8 workers, custom config)

Deploy JobPost analytics service:

# Deploy analytics service with ML capabilities
docker run -d \
    -p 8095:8095 \
    --name jobpost-analytics-ml \
    -e DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.analytics \
    -e ML_MODELS_ENABLED=true \
    -e ANALYTICS_WORKERS=8 \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:ml-v4.0

# Test ML-powered job analytics
curl -X POST "http://localhost:8095/api/analytics/predict-salary/" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "job_title": "Senior Django Developer",
      "skills": ["Python", "Django", "PostgreSQL", "Docker"],
      "location": "San Francisco",
      "experience_years": 5,
      "company_size": "startup"
    }'
{ "prediction": { "salary_range": { "min": 135000, "max": 175000, "median": 155000, "confidence": 0.87 }, "ml_model_info": { "model_type": "Random Forest Regressor", "features_used": 15, "training_data_points": 15420, "model_version": "v4.0", "custom_builder": "anihpj-jobpost-analytics" }, "market_insights": { "skill_demand_multiplier": { "Python": 1.15, "Django": 1.12, "PostgreSQL": 1.08, "Docker": 1.18 }, "location_premium": 1.35, "experience_factor": 1.42 } }, "analytics_metadata": { "processing_time_ms": 234, "parallel_workers": 8, "cache_utilization": "78%", "build_optimizations": "custom_config_enabled" } }

Test advanced JobPost analytics:

# Run comprehensive job market analysis
curl -X POST "http://localhost:8095/api/analytics/market-trends/" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "analysis_type": "skill_demand_forecast",
      "time_horizon": "6_months",
      "geographic_scope": "bay_area",
      "job_categories": ["tech", "engineering"]
    }'
{ "forecast": { "skill_trends": [ { "skill": "Python", "current_demand": 95, "predicted_demand": 98, "growth_rate": 3.2, "job_openings_forecast": 1250 }, { "skill": "Machine Learning", "current_demand": 78, "predicted_demand": 89, "growth_rate": 14.1, "job_openings_forecast": 890 }, { "skill": "Docker", "current_demand": 82, "predicted_demand": 91, "growth_rate": 11.0, "emerging_trend": "container_orchestration" } ], "ml_analysis": { "model_accuracy": 0.89, "prediction_confidence": "high", "data_sources": 15420, "algorithm": "ensemble_learning", "custom_builder_performance": "8x parallelism" } } }

JobPost Model ML Analytics Operations:

# Test JobPost model with ML analytics builder
docker exec -it jobpost-analytics-ml python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import JobPost, Skills, Author, Location, User
from django.db.models import Count, Avg, Q, F
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
import numpy as np
import os

# ML Analytics environment
print("JobPost Analytics - ML Builder Configuration")
print(f"ML Models: {os.getenv('ML_MODELS_ENABLED', 'true')}")
print(f"Analytics Workers: {os.getenv('ANALYTICS_WORKERS', '8')}")
print(f"Builder: anihpj-jobpost-analytics (custom config)")

# JobPost ML feature engineering
ml_features = JobPost.objects.select_related('location', 'author').prefetch_related('skills').annotate(
    skills_count=Count('skills'),
    location_job_density=Count('location__jobpost'),
    author_experience=Count('author__jobpost')
).filter(salary_max__isnull=False, salary_min__isnull=False)

print(f"ML Training Dataset: {ml_features.count()} jobs")

# Advanced analytics queries
salary_analytics = ml_features.aggregate(
    avg_salary=Avg(F('salary_min') + F('salary_max')) / 2,
    min_salary=Min('salary_min'),
    max_salary=Max('salary_max'),
    skill_diversity=Avg('skills_count')
)

print("Salary Analytics:")
for metric, value in salary_analytics.items():
    print(f"  {metric}: {value:.2f}" if isinstance(value, float) else f"  {metric}: {value}")

# ML model performance simulation
high_demand_skills = Skills.objects.filter(demand_score__gte=85)
print(f"High-Demand Skills for ML: {high_demand_skills.count()}")

for skill in high_demand_skills[:5]:
    job_count = JobPost.objects.filter(skills=skill).count()
    print(f"  {skill.name}: {job_count} jobs (demand: {skill.demand_score})")
JobPost Analytics - ML Builder Configuration ML Models: true Analytics Workers: 8 Builder: anihpj-jobpost-analytics (custom config) ML Training Dataset: 892 jobs Salary Analytics: avg_salary: 142750.00 min_salary: 45000 max_salary: 350000 skill_diversity: 3.40 High-Demand Skills for ML: 8 Python: 189 jobs (demand: 95) Machine Learning: 156 jobs (demand: 92) Django: 178 jobs (demand: 88) Docker: 134 jobs (demand: 85) Kubernetes: 89 jobs (demand: 85) JobPost QuerySet: , , , , ]>

docker buildx imagetools

Official Docs: docker buildx imagetools | Docker CLI: docker buildx imagetools

Inspect and manipulate multi-platform container images and manifests for advanced Django deployment scenarios, registry management, and cross-platform compatibility verification.

Full Description

The docker buildx imagetools command provides advanced image inspection and manipulation capabilities for multi-platform container images. For your anihpj Django project, imagetools is essential when managing complex deployment scenarios involving multiple architectures, validating cross-platform builds, creating custom multi-platform manifests, and troubleshooting registry-related issues.

Imagetools enables Django development teams to inspect manifest lists, verify platform-specific image layers, create custom multi-platform images from existing single-platform builds, and manage registry metadata. This is particularly valuable for Django applications deployed across different cloud providers, development environments with mixed architectures (Intel and ARM), and CI/CD pipelines requiring detailed image validation and manipulation.

Syntax

# Inspect multi-platform image manifest
docker buildx imagetools inspect {image-ref}

# Create multi-platform manifest from existing images
docker buildx imagetools create -t {target-ref} {source-ref1} {source-ref2}

# Inspect specific platform image
docker buildx imagetools inspect --format json {image-ref}

# Create manifest with specific tags
docker buildx imagetools create -t {target}:latest -t {target}:v1.0 {source}

# Inspect and extract platform details
docker buildx imagetools inspect --raw {image-ref}

# Create from multi-platform sources
docker buildx imagetools create -t {target} {source}@{digest1} {source}@{digest2}

# Inspect with custom output format
docker buildx imagetools inspect --format '{{ .Manifest.MediaType }}' {image-ref}

Parameters Description

  • inspect: Display detailed information about image manifests, platforms, and metadata. Essential for Django deployment validation and troubleshooting multi-platform builds.
  • create: Create new multi-platform manifests by combining existing images. Useful for Django applications when building platform-specific images separately and combining them.
  • -t, --tag: Tag names for the created manifest. Supports multiple tags for Django versioning strategies and deployment environment labeling.
  • --format: Output format for inspect command (json, raw, or template). Enables automated Django deployment pipeline integration and custom analysis tools.
  • --raw: Display raw manifest JSON without formatting. Provides complete manifest details for Django deployment automation and registry debugging.
  • --dry-run: Show what would be created without actually creating the manifest. Useful for Django deployment validation and testing manifest creation logic.
  • --append: Add platform images to existing multi-platform manifest instead of replacing. Enables incremental Django multi-platform deployment strategies.
  • --platform: Filter inspect output to specific platforms. Helps Django teams focus on relevant architecture-specific information during deployment verification.

Attributes

  • Manifest Type: Identifies whether image is single-platform or multi-platform manifest list for Django deployment planning.
  • Platform Details: Architecture, OS, and variant information for each platform in Django cross-platform deployments.
  • Layer Information: Digests, sizes, and metadata for image layers enabling Django deployment optimization and troubleshooting.
  • Registry Metadata: Creation time, labels, annotations, and other registry-specific information for Django image management.
  • Digest References: Immutable content-addressable identifiers for precise Django deployment targeting and version control.

Methods

Inspect Multi-Platform Manifest

Syntax: docker buildx imagetools inspect {image-ref}

Parameters: image-ref (string) - Registry reference to multi-platform image

Analyze Django application images for platform compatibility, layer structure, and deployment readiness across architectures.

Create Combined Manifest

Syntax: docker buildx imagetools create -t {target} {source1} {source2}

Parameters: target (string) - New manifest reference, sources (strings) - Platform-specific image references

Combine separately built Django platform images into unified multi-platform deployment manifest for registry distribution.

Extract Platform-Specific Image

Syntax: docker buildx imagetools inspect --platform {platform} {image-ref}

Parameters: platform (string) - Target platform specification, image-ref (string) - Multi-platform image reference

Extract specific platform details from Django multi-platform images for targeted deployment and debugging scenarios.

Return Value

Returns detailed manifest information, platform specifications, and image metadata. For inspect operations, provides manifest structure, layer details, and platform availability. Create operations return new manifest digest and reference information.

  • Success (0): Operation completed successfully with valid manifest data
  • Error (1): Image not found or insufficient permissions for registry access
  • Error (2): Invalid manifest format or corrupted image data

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills API Multi-Platform Deployment Verification

Inspect Skills API multi-platform image to verify all required architectures are available and properly configured for deployment across different infrastructure providers and development environments.

# Inspect Skills API multi-platform manifest
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/skills-api:multiarch
Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json Digest: sha256:abcdef123456789... Manifests: Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch@sha256:111222333444... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/amd64 Size: 245.3MB Layers: 12 Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch@sha256:555666777888... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/arm64 Size: 238.7MB Layers: 12 Created: 2026-01-11T15:30:00Z Labels: - django.version=6.0 - anihpj.service=skills-api - anihpj.tier=backend

Get detailed JSON manifest for automation:

# Get JSON format for CI/CD pipeline
docker buildx imagetools inspect --format json anihpj/skills-api:multiarch | jq '.manifests[]'
{ "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json", "size": 1234, "digest": "sha256:111222333444...", "platform": { "architecture": "amd64", "os": "linux" } } { "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json", "size": 1198, "digest": "sha256:555666777888...", "platform": { "architecture": "arm64", "os": "linux" } }

Verify platform-specific Skills API deployment:

# Check AMD64 specific image for cloud deployment
docker buildx imagetools inspect --platform linux/amd64 anihpj/skills-api:multiarch

# Deploy and test Skills API on AMD64
docker run --platform linux/amd64 -d -p 8000:8000 --name skills-amd64 anihpj/skills-api:multiarch
curl http://localhost:8000/api/skills/platform-info/
# AMD64 Platform Details: Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch@sha256:111222333444... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/amd64 Size: 245.3MB Config: Digest: sha256:config111... Size: 7.2KB Layers: - sha256:layer111... (45.2MB) - Python 3.11 base - sha256:layer222... (89.1MB) - Django dependencies - sha256:layer333... (23.4MB) - Application code - sha256:layer444... (2.8MB) - Static files - sha256:layer555... (1.2MB) - Configuration # Skills API Platform Response: { "platform": { "architecture": "amd64", "os": "linux", "python_version": "3.11.0", "django_version": "6.0" }, "container_info": { "image_digest": "sha256:111222333444...", "image_size_mb": 245.3, "layer_count": 12 }, "deployment_ready": true }

Skills Model Platform Compatibility Test:

# Test Skills model cross-platform compatibility
docker exec -it skills-amd64 python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Skills, JobPost, Author, Location, User
import platform
import sys

# Platform verification
print(f"Container Platform: {platform.platform()}")
print(f"Python Architecture: {platform.machine()}")
print(f"System Info: {sys.version}")

# Skills model cross-platform operations
skills_by_platform = {
    'total_skills': Skills.objects.count(),
    'platform': platform.machine(),
    'python_version': platform.python_version(),
    'architecture_optimized': platform.machine() == 'x86_64'
}

print("Skills Platform Analysis:", skills_by_platform)

# Test cross-platform Skills queries
platform_skills = Skills.objects.filter(
    Q(name__icontains='docker') | Q(name__icontains='kubernetes')
).order_by('name')

print("Platform-Related Skills:")
for skill in platform_skills:
    print(f"  {skill.name} (demand: {skill.demand_score})")
Container Platform: Linux-6.2.0-x86_64-with-glibc2.36 Python Architecture: x86_64 System Info: 3.11.0 (main, Dec 7 2023, 12:00:00) [GCC 11.2.0] on linux Skills Platform Analysis: {'total_skills': 25, 'platform': 'x86_64', 'python_version': '3.11.0', 'architecture_optimized': True} Platform-Related Skills: Docker (demand: 85) Docker Compose (demand: 78) Kubernetes (demand: 85) Multi-Platform Development (demand: 72) Skills QuerySet: , , , ]>
Example 2: User Authentication Service Manifest Creation

Create a unified multi-platform manifest for User authentication service by combining separately built platform-specific images for optimal CI/CD pipeline integration and deployment flexibility.

Build platform-specific User auth images separately:

# Build AMD64 version for cloud deployment
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 \
    -t anihpj/user-auth:amd64-only \
    --push -f Dockerfile.user-auth .

# Build ARM64 version for Apple Silicon development  
docker buildx build --platform linux/arm64 \
    -t anihpj/user-auth:arm64-only \
    --push -f Dockerfile.user-auth .
[AMD64 Build] [+] Building 32.4s (18/18) FINISHED => pushing layers 6.2s => pushing manifest for docker.io/anihpj/user-auth:amd64-only 0.3s [ARM64 Build] [+] Building 35.7s (18/18) FINISHED => pushing layers 7.1s => pushing manifest for docker.io/anihpj/user-auth:arm64-only 0.3s

Create unified multi-platform manifest:

# Combine platform-specific images into multi-platform manifest
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0 \
    -t anihpj/user-auth:latest \
    anihpj/user-auth:amd64-only \
    anihpj/user-auth:arm64-only
anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0 anihpj/user-auth:latest

Verify created multi-platform manifest:

# Inspect the unified manifest
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0
Name: docker.io/anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0 MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json Digest: sha256:fedcba987654321... Manifests: Name: docker.io/anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0@sha256:amd64digest... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/amd64 Size: 189.4MB Created: 2026-01-11T15:45:12Z Name: docker.io/anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0@sha256:arm64digest... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/arm64 Size: 185.7MB Created: 2026-01-11T15:45:15Z Annotations: - org.opencontainers.image.created: 2026-01-11T15:45:20Z - anihpj.build.method: imagetools-create - anihpj.service: user-authentication

Test unified User auth deployment:

# Deploy unified image - Docker will select appropriate platform
docker run -d -p 8001:8001 --name user-auth-unified anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0

# Test authentication service
curl -X POST http://localhost:8001/api/auth/register/ \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "username": "unified_user", 
      "email": "unified@example.com",
      "password": "secure123"
    }'
{ "success": true, "user": { "id": 12, "username": "unified_user", "email": "unified@example.com", "date_joined": "2026-01-11T15:50:00Z", "is_active": true }, "deployment_info": { "image_type": "unified_multiplatform", "platform_selected": "linux/amd64", "manifest_digest": "sha256:fedcba987654321...", "build_method": "imagetools_create" }, "message": "User registered with unified multi-platform image" }

Compare platform performance:

# Test ARM64 deployment on Apple Silicon
docker run --platform linux/arm64 -d -p 8002:8001 --name user-auth-arm64 anihpj/user-auth:unified-v2.0

# Performance comparison
curl http://localhost:8001/api/auth/performance-test/
curl http://localhost:8002/api/auth/performance-test/
# AMD64 Performance: { "platform": "linux/amd64", "response_time_ms": 145, "memory_usage_mb": 89.2, "cpu_optimization": "x86_optimized", "database_queries": 3, "cache_hits": 78 } # ARM64 Performance: { "platform": "linux/arm64", "response_time_ms": 132, "memory_usage_mb": 82.4, "cpu_optimization": "arm_optimized", "database_queries": 3, "cache_hits": 81 }

User Model Multi-Platform Analysis:

# Compare User model performance across platforms
docker exec -it user-auth-unified python manage.py shell

from django.contrib.auth.models import User
from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, JobPost, Location
import platform
import time

# Platform analysis
print(f"Unified Deployment Platform: {platform.machine()}")
print(f"Image Source: Imagetools Create")

# User authentication performance test
start = time.time()
users = User.objects.select_related('author').prefetch_related('author__skills')
user_count = users.count()
query_time = time.time() - start

print(f"User Query Performance: {query_time:.4f}s for {user_count} users")

# Cross-platform User analytics
platform_users = User.objects.annotate(
    author_posts=Count('author__jobpost', distinct=True),
    author_skills=Count('author__skills', distinct=True)
).filter(is_active=True)

print("User Platform Analytics:")
for user in platform_users[:5]:
    print(f"  {user.username}: {user.author_posts} posts, {user.author_skills} skills")
Unified Deployment Platform: x86_64 Image Source: Imagetools Create User Query Performance: 0.0156s for 12 users User Platform Analytics: unified_user: 0 posts, 0 skills admin: 5 posts, 8 skills tech_lead: 12 posts, 15 skills content_author: 8 posts, 6 skills api_user: 3 posts, 4 skills User QuerySet: , , , , ]>
Example 3: Author Content Service Registry Analysis

Perform comprehensive registry analysis of Author content service images to identify optimization opportunities, verify layer sharing, and troubleshoot deployment issues across different environments.

# Get raw manifest data for Author service analysis
docker buildx imagetools inspect --raw anihpj/author-service:latest > author-manifest.json

# Extract platform-specific layer information
docker buildx imagetools inspect --format '{{json .}}' anihpj/author-service:latest | jq -r '.manifests[] | "Platform: \(.platform.os)/\(.platform.architecture), Size: \(.size), Digest: \(.digest)"'
Platform: linux/amd64, Size: 1456, Digest: sha256:abc123def456... Platform: linux/arm64, Size: 1423, Digest: sha256:ghi789jkl012...

Analyze layer sharing and optimization:

# Compare layer details between platforms
docker buildx imagetools inspect --platform linux/amd64 anihpj/author-service:latest --format '{{range .Layers}}{{.Digest}} {{.Size}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'

docker buildx imagetools inspect --platform linux/arm64 anihpj/author-service:latest --format '{{range .Layers}}{{.Digest}} {{.Size}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'
# AMD64 Layers: sha256:layer001... 32145678 sha256:layer002... 89234567 sha256:layer003... 12345678 sha256:layer004... 45678901 sha256:layer005... 23456789 sha256:layer006... 8901234 sha256:layer007... 5678912 sha256:layer008... 3456789 # ARM64 Layers: sha256:layer001... 32145678 # Shared layer sha256:layer002... 89234567 # Shared layer sha256:layer009... 12389045 # Platform-specific sha256:layer010... 45623478 # Platform-specific sha256:layer005... 23456789 # Shared layer sha256:layer011... 8934512 # Platform-specific sha256:layer007... 5678912 # Shared layer sha256:layer012... 3489067 # Platform-specific Layer Sharing Analysis: - Shared layers: 4/8 (50%) - Platform-specific layers: 4/8 (50%) - Total size reduction: ~40% through layer sharing

Create optimized Author service manifest:

# Extract specific digests for optimized deployment
AMD64_DIGEST=$(docker buildx imagetools inspect --platform linux/amd64 anihpj/author-service:latest --format '{{.Name}}@{{.Digest}}')
ARM64_DIGEST=$(docker buildx imagetools inspect --platform linux/arm64 anihpj/author-service:latest --format '{{.Name}}@{{.Digest}}')

# Create optimized manifest with specific digests
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5 \
    -t anihpj/author-service:production \
    $AMD64_DIGEST \
    $ARM64_DIGEST
anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5 anihpj/author-service:production

Verify optimization results:

# Compare original vs optimized manifest
echo "=== Original Manifest ==="
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/author-service:latest | head -15

echo "=== Optimized Manifest ==="  
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5 | head -15

Manifests: Name: docker.io/anihpj/author-service:latest@sha256:amd64original... Platform: linux/amd64 Size: 234.5MB Name: docker.io/anihpj/author-service:latest@sha256:arm64original... Platform: linux/arm64 Size: 228.9MB === Optimized Manifest === Name: docker.io/anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5 MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json Digest: sha256:optimized456... Manifests: Name: docker.io/anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5@sha256:amd64optimized... Platform: linux/amd64 Size: 234.5MB Name: docker.io/anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5@sha256:arm64optimized... Platform: linux/arm64 Size: 228.9MB

Deploy and test optimized Author service:

# Deploy optimized Author service
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name author-optimized anihpj/author-service:optimized-v1.5

# Test Author content management
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/authors/content/ \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "title": "Registry Optimization Techniques",
      "content": "Advanced Docker imagetools usage for content management",
      "author_id": 1,
      "tags": ["docker", "optimization", "registry"]
    }'
{ "success": true, "content": { "id": 35, "title": "Registry Optimization Techniques", "slug": "registry-optimization-techniques", "author": { "id": 1, "username": "optimization_author" }, "optimization_info": { "manifest_type": "optimized", "layer_sharing": "50%", "deployment_efficiency": "improved", "imagetools_created": true }, "created_at": "2026-01-11T16:05:00Z" } }

Author Model Registry Optimization Analysis:

# Analyze Author model with registry-optimized deployment
docker exec -it author-optimized python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, JobPost, User, Location
from django.db.models import Count, Q
import os

# Registry optimization analysis
print("Author Service - Registry Optimization")
print(f"Manifest Type: Optimized with imagetools")
print(f"Layer Sharing: 50% efficiency gain")

# Author content optimization metrics
content_authors = Author.objects.annotate(
    content_count=Count('jobpost'),
    optimization_content=Count('jobpost', filter=Q(jobpost__title__icontains='optimization'))
).filter(content_count__gt=0)

print("Content Authors (Registry Optimized):")
for author in content_authors.order_by('-content_count')[:5]:
    print(f"  {author.username}: {author.content_count} posts, {author.optimization_content} optimization posts")

# Registry efficiency analysis
registry_metrics = {
    'total_authors': Author.objects.count(),
    'active_content_authors': content_authors.count(),
    'layer_sharing_efficiency': 0.50,
    'deployment_optimization': 'imagetools_manifest'
}

print("Registry Efficiency Metrics:", registry_metrics)

# Author-skill optimization relationships  
optimization_skills = Skills.objects.filter(
    Q(name__icontains='docker') | Q(name__icontains='optimization')
)

for skill in optimization_skills:
    author_count = Author.objects.filter(jobpost__skills=skill).distinct().count()
    print(f"Optimization Skill: {skill.name} ({author_count} authors)")
Author Service - Registry Optimization Manifest Type: Optimized with imagetools Layer Sharing: 50% efficiency gain Content Authors (Registry Optimized): optimization_author: 15 posts, 3 optimization posts tech_lead: 12 posts, 2 optimization posts content_specialist: 10 posts, 1 optimization posts platform_expert: 8 posts, 2 optimization posts registry_admin: 6 posts, 4 optimization posts Registry Efficiency Metrics: {'total_authors': 18, 'active_content_authors': 12, 'layer_sharing_efficiency': 0.5, 'deployment_optimization': 'imagetools_manifest'} Optimization Skill: Docker (8 authors) Optimization Skill: Docker Optimization (4 authors) Optimization Skill: Registry Management (3 authors) Author QuerySet: , , , , ]>
Example 4: Location Service Geographic Platform Distribution

Analyze and optimize Location service deployment across different geographic regions and platforms, ensuring optimal performance for GIS operations and location-based job search functionality.

# Inspect Location service for geographic deployment optimization
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/location-service:geo-v3.0 --format json | jq -r '.manifests[] | {platform: .platform, size: .size, digest: .digest}'
{ "platform": { "architecture": "amd64", "os": "linux" }, "size": 1567, "digest": "sha256:geo-amd64-abc123..." } { "platform": { "architecture": "arm64", "os": "linux" }, "size": 1534, "digest": "sha256:geo-arm64-def456..." }

Create region-specific Location service manifests:

# Create US West deployment manifest (AMD64 optimized for cloud)
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/location-service:us-west \
    anihpj/location-service:geo-v3.0@sha256:geo-amd64-abc123...

# Create EU deployment manifest (ARM64 for edge computing)
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/location-service:eu-central \
    anihpj/location-service:geo-v3.0@sha256:geo-arm64-def456...

# Create global manifest for auto-platform selection
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/location-service:global \
    anihpj/location-service:geo-v3.0
anihpj/location-service:us-west anihpj/location-service:eu-central anihpj/location-service:global

Verify geographic deployment configurations:

# Test US West deployment (AMD64)
docker run -d -p 8090:8090 --name location-us-west \
    -e GEOGRAPHIC_REGION=us-west \
    -e DEPLOYMENT_ZONE=cloud-amd64 \
    anihpj/location-service:us-west

# Test EU Central deployment (ARM64)  
docker run --platform linux/arm64 -d -p 8091:8090 --name location-eu-central \
    -e GEOGRAPHIC_REGION=eu-central \
    -e DEPLOYMENT_ZONE=edge-arm64 \
    anihpj/location-service:eu-central

Test geographic performance optimization:

# Test US West location services
curl "http://localhost:8090/api/locations/nearby/?lat=37.7749&lon=-122.4194&radius=25" | jq '.performance'

# Test EU Central location services  
curl "http://localhost:8091/api/locations/nearby/?lat=52.5200&lon=13.4050&radius=25" | jq '.performance'
# US West Performance (AMD64): { "response_time_ms": 156, "platform": "linux/amd64", "geographic_region": "us-west", "deployment_zone": "cloud-amd64", "gis_optimization": "cloud-tuned", "locations_found": 15, "cache_efficiency": "82%" } # EU Central Performance (ARM64): { "response_time_ms": 134, "platform": "linux/arm64", "geographic_region": "eu-central", "deployment_zone": "edge-arm64", "gis_optimization": "edge-tuned", "locations_found": 12, "cache_efficiency": "87%" }

Create comprehensive global deployment analysis:

# Analyze global deployment readiness
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/location-service:global --format '{{.Name}} - Platforms: {{range .Manifests}}{{.Platform.OS}}/{{.Platform.Architecture}} {{end}}'

# Test automatic platform selection
docker run -d -p 8092:8090 --name location-global \
    -e GEOGRAPHIC_REGION=auto \
    -e DEPLOYMENT_ZONE=adaptive \
    anihpj/location-service:global
docker.io/anihpj/location-service:global - Platforms: linux/amd64 linux/arm64 Creating location-global... Platform auto-selected: linux/amd64 (host architecture match)

Test global location service coordination:

# Test cross-region location coordination
curl -X POST "http://localhost:8092/api/locations/global-search/" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "regions": ["us-west", "eu-central"],
      "job_criteria": {
        "remote_friendly": true,
        "skills": ["Django", "GIS", "Python"]
      },
      "coordination": "multi-platform"
    }'
{ "global_search_results": { "us_west": { "locations": [ { "name": "San Francisco Bay Area", "jobs": 156, "platform": "linux/amd64", "response_time_ms": 145 }, { "name": "Seattle Tech Hub", "jobs": 89, "platform": "linux/amd64", "response_time_ms": 134 } ] }, "eu_central": { "locations": [ { "name": "Berlin Tech District", "jobs": 67, "platform": "linux/arm64", "response_time_ms": 128 }, { "name": "Amsterdam Innovation Quarter", "jobs": 45, "platform": "linux/arm64", "response_time_ms": 142 } ] }, "coordination": { "total_locations": 4, "total_jobs": 357, "multi_platform": true, "global_manifest": "anihpj/location-service:global" } } }

Location Model Geographic Platform Analysis:

# Analyze Location model with geographic platform optimization
docker exec -it location-global python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Location, JobPost, Skills, Author, User
from django.contrib.gis.geos import Point
from django.contrib.gis.measure import Distance  
from django.db.models import Count, Q
import platform
import os

# Geographic platform analysis
print(f"Global Location Service Platform: {platform.machine()}")
print(f"Geographic Region: {os.getenv('GEOGRAPHIC_REGION', 'auto')}")
print(f"Deployment Zone: {os.getenv('DEPLOYMENT_ZONE', 'adaptive')}")

# Location distribution analysis
global_locations = Location.objects.annotate(
    job_count=Count('jobpost'),
    remote_jobs=Count('jobpost', filter=Q(jobpost__remote_friendly=True)),
    tech_jobs=Count('jobpost', filter=Q(jobpost__skills__category='Programming'))
).filter(job_count__gt=0)

print(f"Global Locations: {global_locations.count()}")

# Geographic clustering analysis
us_locations = global_locations.filter(
    point__longitude__lte=-100  # Rough US boundary
)
eu_locations = global_locations.filter(
    point__longitude__gte=0,
    point__longitude__lte=30,  # Rough EU boundary  
    point__latitude__gte=40,
    point__latitude__lte=60
)

print("Geographic Distribution:")
print(f"  US Locations: {us_locations.count()}")  
print(f"  EU Locations: {eu_locations.count()}")

# Platform-optimized location queries
print("Top Locations by Region:")
for region, qs in [("US", us_locations), ("EU", eu_locations)]:
    top_locations = qs.order_by('-job_count')[:3]
    print(f"  {region}:")
    for loc in top_locations:
        print(f"    {loc.name}: {loc.job_count} jobs ({loc.remote_jobs} remote)")
Global Location Service Platform: x86_64 Geographic Region: auto Deployment Zone: adaptive Global Locations: 28 Geographic Distribution: US Locations: 15 EU Locations: 8 Top Locations by Region: US: San Francisco: 245 jobs (78 remote) Seattle: 189 jobs (89 remote) Austin: 156 jobs (67 remote) EU: Berlin: 134 jobs (56 remote) Amsterdam: 89 jobs (34 remote) London: 167 jobs (78 remote) Location QuerySet: , , , , ]>
Example 5: JobPost Analytics Advanced Manifest Management

Implement advanced manifest management for JobPost analytics service including version control, rollback capabilities, and A/B testing across different platform configurations for machine learning workloads.

Create versioned JobPost analytics manifests:

# Create baseline analytics manifest
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:baseline-v4.0 \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:stable \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:ml-v4.0

# Create experimental ML variant
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1 \
    --build-arg TENSORFLOW_VERSION=2.14.0 \
    --build-arg EXPERIMENTAL_FEATURES=true \
    --push -f Dockerfile.jobpost-analytics-experimental .

# Create A/B testing manifest combining versions
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:ab-test-v4 \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:baseline-v4.0@$(docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/jobpost-analytics:baseline-v4.0 --format '{{.Digest}}') \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1@$(docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1 --format '{{.Digest}}')
[Experimental Build] [+] Building 95.3s (42/42) FINISHED => pushing manifest for docker.io/anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1 0.4s [A/B Manifest Creation] anihpj/jobpost-analytics:ab-test-v4

Implement version control and rollback strategy:

# Create version manifest tracking
cat > analytics-versions.json << 'EOF'
{
  "versions": {
    "v4.0": {
      "manifest": "anihpj/jobpost-analytics:baseline-v4.0",
      "digest": "sha256:baseline123...",
      "features": ["standard_ml", "basic_analytics"],
      "stability": "stable"
    },
    "v4.1": {
      "manifest": "anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1", 
      "digest": "sha256:experimental456...",
      "features": ["advanced_ml", "experimental_analytics", "tensorflow_2.14"],
      "stability": "experimental"
    }
  }
}
EOF

# Function to rollback to specific version
rollback_analytics() {
    VERSION=$1
    MANIFEST=$(jq -r ".versions.\"$VERSION\".manifest" analytics-versions.json)
    docker buildx imagetools create -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:current $MANIFEST
    echo "Rolled back to $VERSION: $MANIFEST"
}

Deploy A/B testing configuration:

# Deploy baseline version (Control)
docker run -d -p 8095:8095 --name analytics-control \
    -e VARIANT=baseline \
    -e ML_VERSION=v4.0 \
    -e ANALYTICS_MODE=control \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:baseline-v4.0

# Deploy experimental version (Test)  
docker run -d -p 8096:8095 --name analytics-test \
    -e VARIANT=experimental \
    -e ML_VERSION=v4.1 \
    -e ANALYTICS_MODE=test \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1

Test A/B performance comparison:

# Test baseline analytics performance
curl -X POST "http://localhost:8095/api/analytics/predict-job-match/" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "skills": ["Python", "Django", "Machine Learning"],
      "experience": 5,
      "location_preference": "remote",
      "salary_expectation": 150000
    }' | jq '.performance'

# Test experimental analytics performance  
curl -X POST "http://localhost:8096/api/analytics/predict-job-match/" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "skills": ["Python", "Django", "Machine Learning"],
      "experience": 5,
      "location_preference": "remote", 
      "salary_expectation": 150000
    }' | jq '.performance'
# Baseline Performance (v4.0): { "processing_time_ms": 456, "model_version": "v4.0", "tensorflow_version": "2.13.0", "accuracy_score": 0.87, "variant": "baseline", "manifest_type": "stable" } # Experimental Performance (v4.1): { "processing_time_ms": 298, "model_version": "v4.1", "tensorflow_version": "2.14.0", "accuracy_score": 0.91, "variant": "experimental", "manifest_type": "experimental", "improvement": "35% faster, 4.6% more accurate" }

Analyze manifest performance and make deployment decision:

# Collect A/B test metrics
echo "=== Performance Comparison ==="
echo "Baseline: 456ms, 87% accuracy"
echo "Experimental: 298ms, 91% accuracy"
echo "Improvement: 35% faster, 4.6% more accurate"

# Promote experimental to production based on results
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:production-v4.1 \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:latest \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:experimental-v4.1

# Create rollback manifest for safety
docker buildx imagetools create \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-analytics:rollback-v4.0 \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:baseline-v4.0
=== Performance Comparison === Baseline: 456ms, 87% accuracy Experimental: 298ms, 91% accuracy Improvement: 35% faster, 4.6% more accurate anihpj/jobpost-analytics:production-v4.1 anihpj/jobpost-analytics:latest anihpj/jobpost-analytics:rollback-v4.0

Deploy promoted production version:

# Deploy promoted production analytics
docker run -d -p 8097:8095 --name analytics-production \
    -e VARIANT=production \
    -e ML_VERSION=v4.1 \
    -e ANALYTICS_MODE=production \
    anihpj/jobpost-analytics:production-v4.1

# Test production deployment
curl "http://localhost:8097/api/analytics/deployment-status/"
{ "deployment_status": "active", "version": "v4.1", "variant": "production", "manifest_info": { "name": "anihpj/jobpost-analytics:production-v4.1", "promoted_from": "experimental-v4.1", "rollback_available": "rollback-v4.0" }, "performance_metrics": { "avg_response_time_ms": 298, "ml_accuracy": 0.91, "improvement_over_baseline": { "speed": "35% faster", "accuracy": "4.6% better" } }, "ab_test_results": { "winner": "experimental", "confidence": 0.95, "test_duration": "production_ready" } }

JobPost Model Advanced Analytics Operations:

# Test JobPost model with advanced manifest management
docker exec -it analytics-production python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import JobPost, Skills, Author, Location, User
from django.db.models import Count, Avg, Q, F
import os
import time

# Advanced analytics deployment analysis
print("JobPost Analytics - Advanced Manifest Management")
print(f"Deployment Version: {os.getenv('ML_VERSION', 'v4.1')}")
print(f"Variant: {os.getenv('VARIANT', 'production')}")
print(f"Analytics Mode: {os.getenv('ANALYTICS_MODE', 'production')}")

# Advanced ML-powered JobPost analytics
start = time.time()
ml_analytics = JobPost.objects.select_related('location', 'author').prefetch_related('skills').annotate(
    skills_count=Count('skills'),
    skill_demand_avg=Avg('skills__demand_score'),
    location_density=Count('location__jobpost')
).filter(
    salary_max__isnull=False,
    skills_count__gte=2
)

analytics_time = time.time() - start
print(f"Advanced Analytics Query: {analytics_time:.4f}s")

# ML-enhanced job matching analytics
high_value_jobs = ml_analytics.filter(
    skill_demand_avg__gte=85,
    salary_max__gte=120000
).order_by('-skill_demand_avg', '-salary_max')

print("High-Value Job Analytics (ML v4.1):")
for job in high_value_jobs[:5]:
    print(f"  {job.title}: {job.skill_demand_avg:.1f} avg demand, ${job.salary_max:,}")

# Manifest deployment metrics
deployment_metrics = {
    'total_jobs_analyzed': ml_analytics.count(),
    'high_value_jobs': high_value_jobs.count(),
    'ml_version': 'v4.1',
    'performance_improvement': '35% faster than v4.0',
    'accuracy_improvement': '4.6% better predictions'
}

print("Advanced Analytics Metrics:", deployment_metrics)
JobPost Analytics - Advanced Manifest Management Deployment Version: v4.1 Variant: production Analytics Mode: production Advanced Analytics Query: 0.0198s High-Value Job Analytics (ML v4.1): Senior ML Engineer: 94.3 avg demand, $350,000 Principal Django Architect: 91.7 avg demand, $285,000 Staff DevOps Engineer: 89.2 avg demand, $275,000 Lead Data Scientist: 88.6 avg demand, $260,000 Senior Full-Stack Engineer: 86.4 avg demand, $245,000 Advanced Analytics Metrics: {'total_jobs_analyzed': 423, 'high_value_jobs': 89, 'ml_version': 'v4.1', 'performance_improvement': '35% faster than v4.0', 'accuracy_improvement': '4.6% better predictions'} JobPost QuerySet: , , , , ]>

docker build (legacy builder)

Official Docs: docker build | Docker CLI: docker build --disable-buildkit

Build Docker images using the legacy Docker builder (BuildKit disabled). Provides compatibility with older Docker versions and specific build scenarios that require the original builder implementation.

Full Description

The docker build --disable-buildkit command constructs Docker images using the original Docker builder engine, bypassing BuildKit optimizations. For your anihpj Django project, this legacy builder provides compatibility when working with older Docker versions, specific enterprise environments, or build scenarios that require the traditional layer-based building approach without advanced BuildKit features.

When building your anihpj Django application with legacy builder, the process reads your Dockerfile sequentially, creates intermediate containers for each instruction, and builds layers using the traditional Docker daemon. This approach is useful for debugging build issues, maintaining compatibility with legacy CI/CD pipelines, or working in environments where BuildKit features are not available or desired.

Syntax

docker build --disable-buildkit [OPTIONS] PATH | URL | -
Parameters
  • --disable-buildkit - Disable BuildKit and use legacy builder
  • --build-arg - Set build-time variables
  • --cache-from - Images to consider as cache sources
  • --file, -f - Name of the Dockerfile
  • --force-rm - Always remove intermediate containers
  • --isolation - Container isolation technology
  • --label - Set metadata for an image
  • --network - Set the networking mode for RUN instructions
  • --no-cache - Do not use cache when building the image
  • --platform - Set platform if server is multi-platform capable
  • --pull - Always attempt to pull a newer version of the image
  • --quiet, -q - Suppress the build output
  • --rm - Remove intermediate containers after successful build (default true)
  • --tag, -t - Name and optionally a tag in 'name:tag' format
  • --target - Set the target build stage
  • --ulimit - Ulimit options
Attributes
  • Builder Engine: Legacy Docker builder (without BuildKit)
  • Cache Strategy: Layer-based caching only
  • Multi-platform: Limited support
  • Build Context: Full context transfer
  • Compatibility: Docker Engine 1.8+
Methods
  • build() - Execute the legacy build process
  • cache() - Manage layer-based cache
  • tag() - Apply tags to built image
  • export() - Save image to tar archive
  • cleanup() - Remove build artifacts
Return Values
  • Success: Image ID and build log
  • Error: Build failure details and exit code
  • Warnings: Deprecated feature notices
  • Cache Stats: Layer reuse information
Examples
Example 1: Basic Legacy Build
# Build Django app using legacy builder
docker build --disable-buildkit -t anihpj:legacy .

# Create legacy-specific Dockerfile
cat > Dockerfile.legacy << 'EOF'
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
EOF

# Build with legacy builder
docker build --disable-buildkit -f Dockerfile.legacy -t anihpj:legacy-v1.0 .
Image built successfully using legacy builder without BuildKit optimizations
Example 2: Legacy Build with Cache Control
# Build with no cache for clean legacy build
docker build --disable-buildkit --no-cache -t anihpj:fresh .

# Use specific cache source for legacy builds
docker build --disable-buildkit --cache-from anihpj:base -t anihpj:cached .

# Build with build arguments for legacy compatibility
docker build --disable-buildkit \
  --build-arg DJANGO_VERSION=4.2 \
  --build-arg PYTHON_VERSION=3.11 \
  -t anihpj:legacy-args .
Legacy build with controlled cache behavior and build-time variables
Example 3: Multi-stage Legacy Build
# Multi-stage Dockerfile for legacy builds
cat > Dockerfile.multistage << 'EOF'
# Build stage
FROM python:3.11 AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --user -r requirements.txt

# Production stage
FROM python:3.11-slim
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /root/.local
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
ENV PATH=/root/.local/bin:$PATH
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
EOF

# Build specific stage with legacy builder
docker build --disable-buildkit --target builder -t anihpj:builder-legacy .
docker build --disable-buildkit -f Dockerfile.multistage -t anihpj:production-legacy .
Multi-stage build using legacy builder with reduced final image size
Example 4: Legacy Build with Network Control
# Build with host network for legacy compatibility
docker build --disable-buildkit --network host -t anihpj:network .

# Build with custom network for legacy builds
docker network create anihpj-build
docker build --disable-buildkit --network anihpj-build -t anihpj:custom-net .

# Build with isolated network (default)
docker build --disable-buildkit --network none -t anihpj:isolated .
Legacy builds with different network configurations for compatibility
Example 5: Legacy Build with Labels and Metadata
# Build with comprehensive labels using legacy builder
docker build --disable-buildkit \
  --label "app.name=anihpj" \
  --label "app.version=1.0.0" \
  --label "app.description=Django Job Portal" \
  --label "app.maintainer=dev@anihpj.com" \
  --label "build.type=legacy" \
  --label "build.date=$(date -u +'%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')" \
  -t anihpj:labeled .

# Build with Git metadata for legacy tracking
docker build --disable-buildkit \
  --label "vcs.ref=$(git rev-parse HEAD)" \
  --label "vcs.url=https://github.com/user/anihpj" \
  --build-arg GIT_COMMIT=$(git rev-parse HEAD) \
  -t anihpj:git-legacy .
Legacy build with comprehensive metadata and version tracking

docker builder prune

Official Docs: docker builder prune | Docker CLI: docker builder prune

Remove build cache and unused builder resources. Cleans up build artifacts, intermediate layers, and cache entries to free up disk space and maintain a clean build environment.

Full Description

The docker builder prune command removes BuildKit build cache and unused builder resources to reclaim disk space. For your anihpj Django project development workflow, this command helps maintain clean build environments by removing cached layers, intermediate build artifacts, and unused cache entries that accumulate during frequent Django application builds and testing cycles.

Regular cache pruning is essential when developing your anihpj Django application with multiple build iterations, dependency updates, or when switching between development branches. The command intelligently preserves active cache while removing stale entries, optimizing storage usage without significantly impacting build performance for your jobpost app and related Django components.

Syntax

docker builder prune [OPTIONS]
Parameters
  • --all, -a - Remove all unused build cache, not just dangling ones
  • --filter - Provide filter values (e.g., 'until=24h')
  • --force, -f - Do not prompt for confirmation
  • --keep-storage - Amount of disk space to keep for cache
Attributes
  • Cache Types: Build cache, layer cache, source cache
  • Storage Impact: Disk space recovery
  • Scope: All builders or specific builder
  • Safety: Preserves active and recent cache
  • Performance: May slow down subsequent builds
Methods
  • prune() - Execute cache cleanup
  • analyze() - Estimate space to be freed
  • filter() - Apply selective pruning
  • preserve() - Maintain essential cache
  • report() - Show cleanup results
Return Values
  • Success: Amount of space reclaimed
  • Cache IDs: List of removed cache entries
  • Preserved: Cache entries kept for performance
  • Statistics: Before/after disk usage
Examples
Example 1: Basic Builder Cache Cleanup
# Clean up build cache interactively
docker builder prune

# Force cleanup without confirmation
docker builder prune --force

# Check current cache usage before cleanup
docker system df
docker builder prune -f
docker system df
Cleaned up build cache, reclaimed 2.5GB of disk space
Example 2: Aggressive Cache Cleanup
# Remove all build cache, including active cache
docker builder prune --all --force

# Clean up cache older than 24 hours
docker builder prune --filter "until=24h" --force

# Clean up cache older than 7 days
docker builder prune --filter "until=168h" --force
Aggressive cleanup removed all unused build cache, including recent entries
Example 3: Selective Cache Cleanup with Storage Limits
# Keep 10GB of cache storage
docker builder prune --keep-storage 10GB --force

# Keep 50% of current cache
CURRENT_SIZE=$(docker system df -v | grep 'Build Cache' | awk '{print $2}')
docker builder prune --keep-storage $((CURRENT_SIZE / 2))

# Keep minimal cache (1GB)
docker builder prune --keep-storage 1GB --all --force
Maintained 10GB cache while cleaning 15GB of older build artifacts
Example 4: Scheduled Cache Maintenance
# Create maintenance script for anihpj project
cat > docker-cleanup.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "Starting Docker build cache cleanup..."
echo "Current cache usage:"
docker system df

echo "Pruning build cache older than 48 hours..."
docker builder prune --filter "until=48h" --force

echo "After cleanup:"
docker system df
EOF

chmod +x docker-cleanup.sh

# Run weekly cleanup
./docker-cleanup.sh
Automated cleanup script removed 3.2GB of build cache older than 48 hours
Example 5: Development Workflow Cache Management
# Development cycle with cache management
echo "Building anihpj Django app..."
docker build -t anihpj:dev .

echo "Current build cache:"
docker system df --format "table {{.Type}}\t{{.TotalCount}}\t{{.Size}}\t{{.Reclaimable}}"

# Clean old cache before major rebuild
echo "Cleaning cache older than 12 hours before production build..."
docker builder prune --filter "until=12h" -f

# Production build with fresh dependencies
echo "Building production image..."
docker build --no-cache -t anihpj:production .

# Post-build cleanup
echo "Final cleanup of development artifacts..."
docker builder prune --filter "label=stage=development" -f
Development workflow maintained efficient cache usage: 8.5GB active, 4.2GB cleaned

docker buildx dap

Official Docs: docker buildx dap | Docker CLI: docker buildx dap

Debug Access Point (DAP) for BuildKit debugging. Provides advanced debugging capabilities for build processes, allowing developers to inspect, attach to, and troubleshoot complex build scenarios.

Syntax

docker buildx dap [COMMAND] [OPTIONS]

Parameters Description

  • --builder: Override configured builder instance for targeted debugging sessions
  • --debug: Enable debug output for comprehensive build process visibility
  • --verbose: Show verbose output for detailed DAP operation logs
Parameters
  • --builder - Override the configured builder instance
  • --debug - Enable debug output
  • --verbose - Show verbose output
Attributes
  • Protocol: Debug Access Protocol
  • Scope: BuildKit debugging and inspection
  • Access Level: Deep build process visibility
  • Compatibility: BuildKit 0.10+
  • Use Case: Advanced debugging scenarios
Methods
  • attach() - Connect to running build process
  • inspect() - Examine build state
  • trace() - Follow execution flow
  • breakpoint() - Set debugging breakpoints
  • analyze() - Performance analysis
Return Values
  • Debug Session: Active debugging connection
  • Build State: Current process information
  • Performance Data: Timing and resource usage
  • Error Details: Detailed failure analysis
Examples
Example 1: DAP Session Initialization
# Initialize DAP session for anihpj build debugging
docker buildx dap --debug

# Check available DAP commands
docker buildx dap --help

# Enable verbose DAP output
docker buildx dap --verbose
DAP session initialized for advanced BuildKit debugging
Example 2: Build Process Debugging
# Start debug session for Django app build
docker buildx dap attach --builder mybuilder

# Debug specific build stage
docker buildx dap inspect --stage dependencies

# Analyze build performance
docker buildx dap trace --build-id abc123
Attached to build process, detailed execution trace available
Example 3: Multi-platform Build Debugging
# Debug cross-platform build issues
docker buildx dap attach --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64

# Inspect platform-specific build stages
docker buildx dap inspect --platform linux/arm64

# Trace ARM64 build execution
docker buildx dap trace --platform linux/arm64
Platform-specific debugging enabled for ARM64 build troubleshooting
Example 4: Cache Debugging with DAP
# Debug cache behavior in Django builds
docker buildx dap inspect --cache-debug

# Analyze cache hit/miss patterns
docker buildx dap trace --cache-analysis

# Debug remote cache issues
docker buildx dap attach --cache-debug --cache-from type=registry
Cache debugging revealed 75% hit rate with optimization opportunities
Example 5: Advanced Build Error Analysis
# Debug failed anihpj build with DAP
docker buildx dap attach --build-failed

# Inspect error context
docker buildx dap inspect --error-analysis

# Get detailed stack trace
docker buildx dap trace --error-stack

# Export debug session for analysis
docker buildx dap export --session-id debug-001
Detailed error analysis exported: dependency conflict in requirements.txt:23

docker buildx dap attach

Official Docs: docker buildx dap attach | Docker CLI: docker buildx dap attach

Attach to an active or completed build process for debugging. Provides real-time access to build execution for monitoring and troubleshooting.

Syntax

docker buildx dap attach [OPTIONS] [BUILD_ID]
Parameters
  • BUILD_ID - Specific build to attach to
  • --builder - Builder instance to attach to
  • --wait - Wait for new builds to attach
  • --follow - Follow build progress
  • --interactive - Enable interactive debugging
Attributes
  • Attachment Type: Real-time or post-mortem
  • Session State: Active monitoring connection
  • Debugging Level: Full build process access
  • Performance Impact: Minimal overhead
  • Compatibility: All BuildKit versions
Methods
  • connect() - Establish debug connection
  • monitor() - Watch build progress
  • step() - Step through execution
  • break() - Pause execution
  • detach() - Disconnect from session
Return Values
  • Session ID: Unique debugging session identifier
  • Build Status: Current execution state
  • Debug Interface: Interactive debugging capabilities
  • Log Stream: Real-time build output
Examples
Example 1: Attach to Running Build
# Start a Django build in background
docker buildx build -t anihpj:debug . &

# Find the build ID
BUILD_ID=$(docker buildx ls --builder mybuilder | grep "running" | awk '{print $1}')

# Attach to the running build
docker buildx dap attach $BUILD_ID --follow
Attached to build abc123, monitoring Django dependency installation
Example 2: Interactive Debugging Session
# Attach with interactive debugging
docker buildx dap attach --interactive --builder mybuilder

# Wait for next build and auto-attach
docker buildx dap attach --wait --follow

# Attach to specific failed build
docker buildx dap attach build-failed-456 --interactive
Interactive debug session started, breakpoints available
Example 3: Multi-stage Build Attachment
# Attach to specific build stage
docker buildx dap attach --stage dependencies

# Monitor stage progression
docker buildx dap attach --follow --stage-verbose

# Attach to final stage only
docker buildx dap attach --stage production --wait
Attached to dependencies stage, pip install progress visible
Example 4: Builder-specific Attachment
# Attach to specific builder instance
docker buildx dap attach --builder anihpj-builder --follow

# Monitor all builds on builder
docker buildx dap attach --builder mybuilder --wait --all

# Attach with builder context
docker buildx dap attach --builder container --interactive
Monitoring all builds on anihpj-builder, 2 active sessions
Example 5: Automated Debugging Workflow
# Create debug monitoring script
cat > debug-monitor.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "Starting automated debug monitoring..."

# Attach to next build with logging
docker buildx dap attach --wait --follow \
  > build-debug.log 2>&1 &

# Monitor for errors
tail -f build-debug.log | grep -E "(ERROR|FAILED)" &

echo "Debug monitoring active, logs in build-debug.log"
EOF

chmod +x debug-monitor.sh
./debug-monitor.sh
Automated debugging active, will attach to next anihpj build

docker buildx dap build

Official Docs: docker buildx dap build | Docker CLI: docker buildx dap build

Build with integrated DAP debugging support. Combines build execution with debugging capabilities for real-time inspection and troubleshooting.

Syntax

docker buildx dap build [OPTIONS] PATH | URL | -
Parameters
  • --debug-on-error - Enable debugging when build fails
  • --breakpoint - Set breakpoint at specific stage
  • --trace-level - Set tracing verbosity level
  • --interactive - Enable interactive debugging mode
  • --save-debug - Save debug session for later analysis
Attributes
  • Build Mode: Debug-enabled build execution
  • Debugging: Real-time build inspection
  • Performance: Slight overhead for debug features
  • Output: Enhanced logging and trace data
  • Recovery: Error state analysis
Methods
  • build_debug() - Execute build with debugging
  • set_breakpoint() - Configure debug stops
  • trace_execution() - Monitor build flow
  • analyze_failure() - Deep error analysis
  • export_session() - Save debug data
Return Values
  • Build Result: Success/failure with debug context
  • Debug Session: Session ID and trace data
  • Performance Metrics: Detailed timing information
  • Error Analysis: Comprehensive failure details
Examples
Example 1: Basic Debug Build
# Build anihpj with debug support
docker buildx dap build --debug-on-error -t anihpj:debug .

# Interactive debug build
docker buildx dap build --interactive -t anihpj:interactive .

# High verbosity debug build
docker buildx dap build --trace-level verbose -t anihpj:verbose .
Debug build completed with trace session saved
Example 2: Build with Breakpoints
# Set breakpoint at pip install stage
docker buildx dap build --breakpoint "RUN pip install" -t anihpj:breakpoint .

# Multiple breakpoints
docker buildx dap build \
  --breakpoint "COPY requirements.txt" \
  --breakpoint "RUN pip install" \
  --breakpoint "COPY . ." \
  -t anihpj:multi-break .
Build paused at pip install stage, interactive debugging available
Example 3: Debug Session Management
# Build with saved debug session
docker buildx dap build --save-debug session-001 -t anihpj:saved .

# Export debug data
docker buildx dap build --export-debug debug-data.json -t anihpj:export .

# Replay debug session
docker buildx dap replay session-001
Debug session session-001 saved, 2.3MB trace data exported
Example 4: Multi-platform Debug Build
# Debug cross-platform build
docker buildx dap build \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  --debug-on-error \
  --trace-level platform \
  -t anihpj:multiplatform .

# Platform-specific debugging
docker buildx dap build \
  --platform linux/arm64 \
  --breakpoint "RUN pip install" \
  -t anihpj:arm64-debug .
Multi-platform debug build with ARM64-specific breakpoint set
Example 5: Advanced Debug Build Workflow
# Comprehensive debug build for Django app
docker buildx dap build \
  --interactive \
  --debug-on-error \
  --trace-level full \
  --save-debug anihpj-debug-$(date +%Y%m%d) \
  --breakpoint "WORKDIR /app" \
  --breakpoint "COPY requirements.txt" \
  --breakpoint "RUN pip install" \
  --breakpoint "COPY . ." \
  --breakpoint "EXPOSE 8000" \
  --build-arg DEBUG_MODE=true \
  -t anihpj:comprehensive-debug .

# Analyze results
docker buildx dap analyze anihpj-debug-$(date +%Y%m%d)
Comprehensive debug build with 5 breakpoints, full trace analysis available

docker buildx debug

Official Docs: docker buildx debug | Docker CLI: docker buildx debug

Debug BuildKit builds with enhanced debugging capabilities. Provides comprehensive build analysis and troubleshooting for complex build scenarios.

Syntax

docker buildx debug [COMMAND] [OPTIONS]
Parameters
  • --builder - Specify builder instance
  • --verbose - Enable verbose debug output
  • --interactive - Start interactive debug session
Attributes
  • Debug Level: Comprehensive build analysis
  • Interactivity: Step-by-step control
  • Scope: Full BuildKit debugging suite
  • Integration: IDE and CLI debugging
  • Output: Structured debug information
Methods
  • start_session() - Initialize debug session
  • step_through() - Execute step by step
  • analyze_build() - Comprehensive analysis
  • inspect_state() - Current build state
  • export_trace() - Export debug information
Return Values
  • Debug Session: Active debugging interface
  • Analysis Report: Build performance insights
  • Error Context: Detailed failure information
  • Trace Data: Execution flow details
Examples
Example 1: Interactive Debug Session
# Start interactive debugging for anihpj
docker buildx debug --interactive --builder mybuilder

# Debug with verbose output
docker buildx debug --verbose

# Debug specific builder
docker buildx debug --builder anihpj-builder --interactive
Interactive debug session started, build execution control available
Example 2: Build Analysis and Profiling
# Analyze build performance
docker buildx debug analyze --build-id abc123

# Profile build execution
docker buildx debug profile --build django-app

# Generate debug report
docker buildx debug report --output anihpj-debug.json
Build analysis complete: 15.2s total, 8.1s in pip install stage
Example 3: Error Investigation
# Debug failed build
docker buildx debug inspect --build-failed def456

# Investigate specific error
docker buildx debug error --stage requirements --line 23

# Export error context
docker buildx debug export-error --format json
Error investigation: ModuleNotFoundError in requirements.txt line 23
Example 4: Advanced Debugging Workflow
# Comprehensive debug workflow
docker buildx debug start --build anihpj:latest
docker buildx debug breakpoint --stage "RUN pip install"
docker buildx debug step --count 5
docker buildx debug inspect --variables
docker buildx debug continue
docker buildx debug export --session debug-001
Debug workflow executed: 5 steps analyzed, session exported
Example 5: Multi-platform Debug Analysis
# Debug cross-platform issues
docker buildx debug platform --compare linux/amd64,linux/arm64

# Platform-specific analysis
docker buildx debug analyze --platform linux/arm64 --verbose

# Export platform comparison
docker buildx debug export-comparison --platforms all
Platform analysis: ARM64 build 23% slower, optimization opportunities identified

docker buildx debug build

Official Docs: docker buildx debug build | Docker CLI: docker buildx debug build

Execute builds with integrated debugging support. Combines build execution with real-time debugging capabilities for comprehensive build analysis.

Syntax

docker buildx debug build [OPTIONS] PATH | URL | -
Parameters
  • --debug-interactive - Enable interactive debug mode
  • --debug-level - Set debug verbosity (info, debug, trace)
  • --profile - Enable build profiling
  • --trace-file - Save trace to file
  • --breakpoint-on-error - Break on build errors
Attributes
  • Execution Mode: Debug-enabled build process
  • Profiling: Performance analysis integration
  • Tracing: Complete execution trace
  • Error Handling: Enhanced error debugging
  • Interactive Control: Build process manipulation
Methods
  • debug_build() - Execute debug build
  • profile_execution() - Analyze performance
  • trace_steps() - Track build steps
  • handle_errors() - Debug build failures
  • export_data() - Save debug information
Return Values
  • Build Status: Success/failure with debug context
  • Profile Data: Performance metrics
  • Trace Information: Step-by-step execution log
  • Debug Session: Interactive debugging capabilities
Examples
Example 1: Interactive Debug Build
# Interactive debug build for Django app
docker buildx debug build --debug-interactive -t anihpj:debug-interactive .

# High verbosity debug build
docker buildx debug build --debug-level trace -t anihpj:trace .

# Profile-enabled debug build
docker buildx debug build --profile --trace-file anihpj-trace.json -t anihpj:profile .
Interactive debug build started, breakpoints available at each stage
Example 2: Error-focused Debug Build
# Break on any build error
docker buildx debug build --breakpoint-on-error -t anihpj:error-debug .

# Debug with comprehensive error analysis
docker buildx debug build \
  --debug-level debug \
  --breakpoint-on-error \
  --trace-file error-trace.json \
  -t anihpj:error-analysis .

# Analyze previous failed build
docker buildx debug build --from-failed abc123 --debug-interactive
Build paused at error: ModuleNotFoundError, debug shell available
Example 3: Performance Debug Build
# Build with performance profiling
docker buildx debug build \
  --profile \
  --debug-level info \
  --trace-file performance-trace.json \
  -t anihpj:performance .

# Analyze build timing
docker buildx debug analyze-trace performance-trace.json

# Compare with previous builds
docker buildx debug compare --trace1 old-trace.json --trace2 performance-trace.json
Performance analysis: pip install optimized, 40% faster than previous build
Example 4: Multi-stage Debug Build
# Debug specific build stages
docker buildx debug build \
  --debug-interactive \
  --stage-debug dependencies,application \
  --trace-file multi-stage.json \
  -t anihpj:multi-debug .

# Stage-specific profiling
docker buildx debug build \
  --profile \
  --stage-focus dependencies \
  --debug-level debug \
  -t anihpj:stage-focus .
Multi-stage debug: dependencies stage 12.3s, application stage 4.7s
Example 5: Advanced Debug Workflow
# Comprehensive debug build workflow
cat > debug-build.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "Starting comprehensive debug build for anihpj..."

# Stage 1: Interactive debug build
docker buildx debug build \
  --debug-interactive \
  --debug-level trace \
  --profile \
  --trace-file anihpj-debug-$(date +%H%M).json \
  --breakpoint-on-error \
  -t anihpj:debug-$(date +%Y%m%d) .

# Stage 2: Analyze results
echo "Analyzing debug results..."
docker buildx debug analyze-trace anihpj-debug-$(date +%H%M).json

# Stage 3: Generate report
docker buildx debug report --trace anihpj-debug-$(date +%H%M).json --output debug-report.html
EOF

chmod +x debug-build.sh
./debug-build.sh
Comprehensive debug workflow: trace saved, report generated at debug-report.html

docker buildx du

Official Docs: docker buildx du | Docker CLI: docker buildx du

Display disk usage of BuildKit build cache. Shows detailed information about cache consumption, build artifacts, and storage utilization across different builders and platforms.

Full Description

The docker buildx du command provides detailed analysis of BuildKit cache disk usage across all configured builders. For your anihpj Django project, this command is essential for monitoring storage consumption during development cycles, identifying cache bloat from frequent builds, and making informed decisions about cache cleanup to maintain optimal development environment performance.

When managing your anihpj Django application builds, use this command to track cache growth patterns, identify which components consume the most storage, analyze cache efficiency across different builders, and plan storage management strategies. The detailed breakdown helps optimize your development workflow by understanding cache utilization for your jobpost app, user management, and related Django components.

Syntax

# Check current cache usage
docker buildx du

# Detailed analysis with verbose output
docker buildx du --verbose

# JSON format for automation
docker buildx du --format json

# Specific builder analysis
docker buildx du --builder anihpj-builder

# Filter by cache age
docker buildx du --filter "until=24h"

Parameters Description

  • --builder: Specify builder instance for targeted analysis. Essential for multi-builder setups in your anihpj Django project where different builders may have varying cache patterns and storage requirements.
  • --filter: Apply filters to cache entries (until, size, label). Use to analyze specific cache subsets, such as entries older than a certain time or larger than specified sizes, helping optimize your Django build cache strategy.
  • --format: Output format control (table, json, yaml). JSON format enables integration with monitoring scripts and automated cache management tools for your anihpj project infrastructure.
  • --verbose: Show detailed cache breakdown including individual layer information. Provides granular insights into cache composition for your Django application's build layers and dependencies.

Attributes

  • Storage Metrics: Total cache size, active cache, and reclaimable space
  • Cache Analysis: Breakdown by builder, age, and usage patterns
  • Layer Details: Individual cache entry sizes and timestamps
  • Builder Scope: Per-builder cache utilization and comparison
  • Time Tracking: Cache age analysis for cleanup planning

Methods

  • analyze_usage() - Calculate and categorize disk consumption
  • breakdown_cache() - Organize cache by type, age, and builder
  • calculate_efficiency() - Determine cache hit rates and utilization
  • identify_bloat() - Find oversized or stale cache entries
  • generate_report() - Create comprehensive usage analysis

Return Values

  • Total Usage: Overall cache disk consumption across all builders
  • Detailed Breakdown: Per-builder and per-entry cache statistics
  • Efficiency Metrics: Cache utilization rates and effectiveness
  • Cleanup Potential: Estimated space reclaimable through pruning
  • Trend Analysis: Historical cache growth and usage patterns

Syntax

docker buildx du [OPTIONS]
Parameters
  • --builder - Specify builder instance
  • --filter - Filter cache entries
  • --format - Output format (table, json, yaml)
  • --verbose - Show detailed information
Attributes
  • Storage Analysis: Comprehensive disk usage
  • Cache Breakdown: Per-layer and per-build data
  • Builder Scope: Multiple builder support
  • Platform Details: Cross-platform cache usage
  • Time Tracking: Cache age and last access
Methods
  • analyze_usage() - Calculate disk consumption
  • categorize_cache() - Organize by type/age
  • estimate_savings() - Potential cleanup savings
  • track_trends() - Usage over time
  • export_report() - Generate usage reports
Return Values
  • Total Usage: Overall cache disk consumption
  • Cache Details: Individual cache entry sizes
  • Cleanup Potential: Reclaimable space estimates
  • Usage Trends: Historical consumption patterns
Examples
Example 1: Basic Disk Usage Analysis
# Check BuildKit cache usage
docker buildx du

# Detailed cache analysis
docker buildx du --verbose

# JSON format output
docker buildx du --format json | jq .
Total cache usage: 4.2GB (15 cache entries, oldest: 7 days ago)
Example 2: Builder-specific Usage
# Check specific builder usage
docker buildx du --builder anihpj-builder

# Compare builders
docker buildx du --builder mybuilder --verbose
docker buildx du --builder container --verbose

# All builders summary
for builder in $(docker buildx ls --format '{{.Name}}'); do
  echo "Builder: $builder"
  docker buildx du --builder $builder
  echo
done
anihpj-builder: 2.1GB, mybuilder: 1.8GB, container: 0.3GB
Example 3: Filtered Cache Analysis
# Filter by cache age
docker buildx du --filter "until=24h"

# Filter by size
docker buildx du --filter "size>100MB"

# Filter by build context
docker buildx du --filter "context=anihpj" --verbose
Filtered results: 8 cache entries matching criteria, 1.4GB total
Example 4: Cache Usage Monitoring
# Create usage monitoring script
cat > cache-monitor.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
DATESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M)
echo "[$DATESTAMP] Docker BuildX Cache Usage Report" > cache-report-$DATESTAMP.txt

docker buildx du --format json > cache-data-$DATESTAMP.json
docker buildx du --verbose >> cache-report-$DATESTAMP.txt

echo "Report saved: cache-report-$DATESTAMP.txt"
EOF

chmod +x cache-monitor.sh
./cache-monitor.sh
Cache monitoring report generated: cache-report-20260111_1430.txt
Example 5: Django Project Cache Analysis
# Analyze anihpj project cache usage
echo "Analyzing cache usage for anihpj Django project..."

# Overall usage
docker buildx du --verbose

# Calculate cache efficiency
TOTAL_SIZE=$(docker buildx du --format json | jq -r '.Total.Size')
ACTIVE_SIZE=$(docker buildx du --format json | jq -r '.Active.Size')
EFFICIENCY=$((ACTIVE_SIZE * 100 / TOTAL_SIZE))

echo "Cache efficiency: ${EFFICIENCY}% (${ACTIVE_SIZE}/${TOTAL_SIZE})"

# Recommend cleanup if efficiency is low
if [ $EFFICIENCY -lt 50 ]; then
  echo "Recommendation: Run 'docker buildx prune' to improve efficiency"
fi

# Export detailed analysis
docker buildx du --format json > anihpj-cache-analysis.json
anihpj cache analysis: 68% efficiency, 2.8GB active of 4.1GB total

docker buildx history

Official Docs: docker buildx history | Docker CLI: docker buildx history

Manage and analyze BuildKit build history. Provides comprehensive tracking of build executions and performance metrics for optimization.

Full Description

The docker buildx history command provides comprehensive tracking and analysis of BuildKit build executions. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables performance monitoring, build trend analysis, troubleshooting failed builds, and optimization of your development workflow by maintaining detailed records of all build activities including timing, success rates, and resource utilization.

When developing your anihpj Django application, build history becomes invaluable for identifying performance bottlenecks, tracking improvements over time, debugging recurring build issues, and maintaining quality metrics for your jobpost app deployments. The historical data helps optimize cache strategies, identify dependency update impacts, and ensure consistent build performance across development cycles.

Syntax

# Show recent build history
docker buildx history

# Detailed history analysis
docker buildx history --verbose --limit 20

# Export history data
docker buildx history --format json > anihpj-builds.json

# Filter by date range
docker buildx history --filter "since=7d"

# Analyze failed builds
docker buildx history --filter "status=failed"

Parameters Description

  • --builder: Target specific builder instance for history analysis. Essential when managing multiple builders for your anihpj Django project with different purposes (development, staging, production).
  • --format: Control output format (table, json, yaml). JSON format enables automated analysis and integration with monitoring systems tracking your Django application build performance.
  • --limit: Restrict number of history entries returned. Useful for performance when analyzing recent builds or when integrating with scripts monitoring your anihpj project build trends.
  • --filter: Apply selection criteria (since, status, builder). Filter by build age, success/failure status, or specific builders to focus analysis on relevant build subset for your Django workflow.

Attributes

  • Build Records: Complete execution history with timestamps and metadata
  • Performance Metrics: Build duration, cache utilization, and resource usage
  • Success Tracking: Build outcomes and failure analysis capabilities
  • Trend Analysis: Historical patterns and performance evolution
  • Export Support: Data portability for external analysis tools

Methods

  • track_builds() - Monitor and record build execution details
  • analyze_performance() - Calculate metrics and identify trends
  • filter_records() - Apply selection criteria to build history
  • export_data() - Generate reports in various formats
  • compare_builds() - Analyze performance changes over time

Return Values

  • Build Timeline: Chronological record of all build executions
  • Performance Data: Duration, resource usage, and efficiency metrics
  • Success Metrics: Build outcome statistics and reliability data
  • Trend Reports: Historical analysis and pattern identification
  • Diagnostic Info: Failure analysis and troubleshooting data

Syntax

docker buildx history [COMMAND] [OPTIONS]
Parameters
  • --builder - Specify builder instance
  • --format - Output format (table, json, yaml)
  • --limit - Limit number of history entries
  • --filter - Filter history entries
Attributes
  • History Tracking: Complete build execution records
  • Performance Metrics: Build timing and resource usage
  • Trend Analysis: Build performance over time
  • Error Tracking: Historical failure analysis
  • Export Capabilities: Data export and analysis
Methods
  • list_builds() - Show build history
  • analyze_trends() - Performance trend analysis
  • export_data() - Export history data
  • import_data() - Import history records
  • trace_execution() - Detailed build traces
Return Values
  • Build Records: Historical build information
  • Performance Data: Timing and resource metrics
  • Trend Reports: Analysis over time periods
  • Export Files: Data in various formats
Examples
Example 1: View Build History
# Show recent build history
docker buildx history

# Detailed history with metadata
docker buildx history --verbose

# JSON format for analysis
docker buildx history --format json
Build history: 23 builds over 14 days, average duration 2m 34s
Example 2: Filtered History Analysis
# Filter by date range
docker buildx history --filter "since=2024-01-01"

# Filter by build status
docker buildx history --filter "status=failed"

# Filter by builder
docker buildx history --filter "builder=anihpj-builder" --limit 10
Filtered history: 5 failed builds found, investigating common issues
Example 3: Performance Trend Analysis
# Analyze build performance trends
docker buildx history --format json | jq '.[] | {date: .created, duration: .duration}'

# Calculate average build times
docker buildx history --format json | jq '[.[] | .duration] | add / length'

# Find slowest builds
docker buildx history --format json | jq 'sort_by(.duration) | reverse | .[0:5]'
Performance trends: 15% improvement over last month, cache hit rate: 82%
Example 4: Django Project History Tracking
# Track anihpj project build history
echo "Analyzing anihpj Django project build history..."

# Recent builds for this project
