89ms (prod) 📊 Skills: 67ms (dev) → 34ms (prod) 🔒 Security & Compliance Status: 🔴 Development: 60% compliance 🔓 🟡 Testing: 75% compliance 🔓 🟢 Staging: 90% compliance 🔐 🟢 Production: 98% compliance 🔐 🎯 Multi-Environment Management Summary: ✅ 4/5 projects running successfully 📊 All 5 Django models deployed across environments 🚀 Production environment: 98% compliance score ⚡ Performance optimized: prod 3-4x faster than dev 🔧 2 optimization opportunities identified 🛡️ Security levels appropriate per environment

docker compose cp

Official Docs: docker compose cp | Docker CLI: docker compose cp

Copy files between Django service containers and local filesystem for data transfer and debugging.

Full Description

The docker compose cp command copies files and directories between your anihpj Django service containers and the local filesystem. This enables data extraction, configuration updates, log retrieval, and debugging workflows by transferring files bidirectionally between containers and host system.

For Django applications, this command is essential for retrieving logs, updating configuration files, extracting database dumps, transferring media files, and debugging issues in your anihpj jobpost application by accessing container filesystem content directly.

Syntax

# Copy file from container to host
docker compose cp web:/app/logs/django.log ./logs/

# Copy file from host to container
docker compose cp ./config.py web:/app/config/

# Copy directory from container
docker compose cp web:/app/media ./backup/media/

# Copy to specific container instance
docker compose cp web-1:/app/db.sqlite3 ./db_backup.sqlite3

# Copy with archive mode
docker compose cp --archive web:/app/static ./static_backup/

# Follow symbolic links
docker compose cp --follow-link web:/app/logs ./logs/

Parameters Description

  • Source Path: Django container path or host path for file source location. Specify origin of files to copy.
  • Destination Path: Django target path for copied files. Specify where files should be placed.
  • --archive: Preserve Django file permissions, ownership, and timestamps during copy operation.
  • --follow-link: Follow Django symbolic links in container filesystem. Resolve symlinks to actual files.
  • Container Instance: Specify Django service container instance for multi-replica deployments.

Attributes

  • Bidirectional Transfer: Copy Django files from container to host or host to container for flexible data management.
  • Directory Support: Transfer entire Django directories and subdirectories with recursive copy operations.
  • Permission Preservation: Maintain Django file permissions and ownership when using archive mode.
  • Real-Time Access: Access Django container filesystem while services are running without interruption.
  • Multi-Service Support: Copy files between different Django services and containers within the same project.

Methods

Log Extraction

Syntax: docker compose cp web:/app/logs/ ./debug_logs/

Parameters: container log directory to host location for Django troubleshooting and analysis

Extract Django application logs, error traces, and debug information for offline analysis and debugging.

Configuration Management

Syntax: docker compose cp ./updated_settings.py web:/app/anihpj/

Parameters: host configuration file to Django container for live updates

Update Django configuration files, environment settings, and application parameters in running containers.

Return Value

Returns success status of Django file copy operation showing transfer completion and any error conditions.

  • Success (0): File copy completed successfully
  • Not Found (1): Source file or directory not found
  • Permission Error (2): Insufficient permissions for copy operation

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Production Debugging & Log Analysis (Critical Issue Investigation)

Extract Django production logs and database files for critical issue investigation involving User authentication failures, JobPost processing errors, Skills verification timeouts, and Location service anomalies requiring offline analysis and forensic debugging.

# Extract Django application logs
docker compose cp web:/app/logs/ ./investigation/logs/

# Copy database for analysis
docker compose cp db:/var/lib/postgresql/data/ ./investigation/db_backup/

# Extract media files for inspection
docker compose cp web:/app/media/ ./investigation/media/

# Copy configuration files
docker compose cp web:/app/anihpj/settings/ ./investigation/config/

Django Models Context & Debugging Investigation:

# Production debugging and log analysis for Django anihpj application
# Investigating User, JobPost, Skills, Author, Location model issues

import os
import json
import datetime
from typing import Dict, List, Any
import shutil

# Critical issue investigation context
investigation_context = {
    "incident_id": "ANIHPJ_CRITICAL_2026_01_12_001",
    "incident_start": "2026-01-12 14:30:00",
    "incident_detection": "2026-01-12 14:35:00",
    "investigation_start": "2026-01-12 14:40:00",
    "severity": "critical",
    "affected_models": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"]
}

# File extraction and analysis plan
extraction_plan = {
    "logs_extraction": {
        "source_paths": [
            "/app/logs/django.log",
            "/app/logs/error.log", 
            "/app/logs/access.log",
            "/app/logs/celery.log",
            "/app/logs/nginx.log"
        ],
        "destination": "./investigation/logs/",
        "priority": "immediate",
        "models_relevance": {
            "django.log": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"],
            "error.log": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills"],
            "celery.log": ["JobPost", "Skills", "Location"],
            "nginx.log": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"]
        }
    },
    "database_analysis": {
        "source_paths": [
            "/var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf",
            "/var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_log/",
            "/var/lib/postgresql/data/base/"
        ],
        "destination": "./investigation/db_analysis/",
        "priority": "high",
        "analysis_focus": [
            "user_authentication_tables",
            "jobpost_search_indexes",
            "skills_verification_logs",
            "location_query_performance"
        ]
    },
    "configuration_audit": {
        "source_paths": [
            "/app/anihpj/settings/production.py",
            "/app/anihpj/urls.py", 
            "/app/anihpj/wsgi.py",
            "/app/requirements.txt",
            "/etc/nginx/nginx.conf"
        ],
        "destination": "./investigation/config/", 
        "priority": "medium",
        "security_analysis": True
    },
    "media_forensics": {
        "source_paths": [
            "/app/media/uploads/",
            "/app/media/avatars/",
            "/app/media/portfolios/",
            "/app/static/admin/"
        ],
        "destination": "./investigation/media/",
        "priority": "low",
        "models_involved": ["User", "Author", "Skills"]
    }
}

# Extracted file analysis results
extracted_files_analysis = {
    "django_log_analysis": {
        "file_size": "2.3GB",
        "lines_count": 4567890,
        "error_entries": 1234,
        "warning_entries": 5678,
        "time_range": "2026-01-10 00:00:00 to 2026-01-12 14:40:00",
        "model_error_breakdown": {
            "User_model_errors": {
                "authentication_failures": 456,
                "session_timeouts": 234,
                "permission_denials": 123,
                "critical_patterns": [
                    "IntegrityError in user creation",
                    "Session key validation failed",
                    "LDAP authentication timeout"
                ]
            },
            "JobPost_model_errors": {
                "search_index_corruption": 89,
                "application_submission_failures": 156,
                "recommendation_timeouts": 78,
                "critical_patterns": [
                    "Elasticsearch connection lost",
                    "Database deadlock in job_post table",
                    "Memory error in recommendation engine"
                ]
            },
            "Skills_model_errors": {
                "verification_api_failures": 234,
                "demand_calculation_errors": 67,
                "cache_miss_cascades": 145,
                "critical_patterns": [
                    "External API rate limit exceeded",
                    "Redis connection timeout",
                    "Skill matching algorithm crash"
                ]
            },
            "Author_model_errors": {
                "portfolio_upload_failures": 45,
                "rating_calculation_errors": 23,
                "availability_sync_issues": 67,
                "critical_patterns": [
                    "S3 bucket access denied",
                    "Rating aggregation SQL timeout",
                    "Calendar API integration failure"
                ]
            },
            "Location_model_errors": {
                "geocoding_api_timeouts": 123,
                "market_analysis_crashes": 34,
                "coordinate_validation_errors": 56,
                "critical_patterns": [
                    "Google Maps API quota exceeded",
                    "PostGIS extension not responding",
                    "Timezone calculation overflow"
                ]
            }
        }
    },
    "database_log_analysis": {
        "postgresql_log_size": "856MB",
        "slow_queries": 345,
        "connection_errors": 67,
        "deadlocks": 12,
        "model_performance_issues": {
            "User_table": {
                "slow_queries": 123,
                "deadlocks": 3,
                "index_usage": "suboptimal",
                "connection_pool": "95%_utilization"
            },
            "JobPost_table": {
                "slow_queries": 156,
                "deadlocks": 5,
                "index_usage": "critical_missing_indexes",
                "full_table_scans": 45
            },
            "Skills_table": {
                "slow_queries": 78,
                "deadlocks": 2,
                "index_usage": "good",
                "cache_hit_rate": "67%"
            }
        }
    },
    "configuration_audit_results": {
        "security_issues": [
            {
                "file": "production.py",
                "issue": "debug_mode_accidentally_enabled",
                "severity": "critical",
                "models_exposed": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"]
            },
            {
                "file": "nginx.conf",
                "issue": "insufficient_rate_limiting",
                "severity": "high", 
                "vulnerability": "api_abuse_possible"
            }
        ],
        "performance_issues": [
            {
                "file": "wsgi.py",
                "issue": "worker_count_misconfiguration",
                "impact": "request_queuing_delays",
                "models_affected": ["User", "JobPost"]
            }
        ]
    }
}

# Investigation findings and root cause analysis
root_cause_analysis = {
    "primary_causes": [
        {
            "cause": "debug_mode_in_production",
            "severity": "critical",
            "discovery_method": "configuration_file_extraction",
            "models_impacted": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"],
            "impact_description": "performance_degradation_and_security_exposure",
            "evidence_files": ["production.py", "django.log"],
            "fix_priority": "immediate"
        },
        {
            "cause": "elasticsearch_cluster_failure",
            "severity": "high",
            "discovery_method": "log_analysis_pattern_matching",
            "models_impacted": ["JobPost", "Skills"],
            "impact_description": "search_functionality_complete_failure",
            "evidence_files": ["django.log", "celery.log"],
            "fix_priority": "urgent"
        },
        {
            "cause": "database_connection_pool_exhaustion",
            "severity": "high",
            "discovery_method": "postgresql_log_analysis",
            "models_impacted": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"],
            "impact_description": "intermittent_service_unavailability",
            "evidence_files": ["postgresql.log", "django.log"],
            "fix_priority": "urgent"
        }
    ],
    "secondary_causes": [
        {
            "cause": "insufficient_nginx_rate_limiting",
            "severity": "medium",
            "models_impacted": ["User", "JobPost"],
            "impact_description": "api_abuse_amplifying_primary_issues"
        }
    ]
}

# Remediation action plan
remediation_plan = {
    "immediate_actions": [
        {
            "action": "disable_debug_mode_in_production",
            "steps": [
                "update_production_settings_file",
                "restart_web_services",
                "verify_debug_disabled"
            ],
            "estimated_time": "5_minutes",
            "models_benefited": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"]
        },
        {
            "action": "restart_elasticsearch_cluster",
            "steps": [
                "diagnose_elasticsearch_failure",
                "restart_elasticsearch_services",
                "rebuild_search_indexes"
            ],
            "estimated_time": "20_minutes",
            "models_benefited": ["JobPost", "Skills"]
        },
        {
            "action": "increase_database_connection_pool",
            "steps": [
                "update_database_configuration",
                "restart_database_service",
                "monitor_connection_metrics"
            ],
            "estimated_time": "10_minutes",
            "models_benefited": ["User", "JobPost", "Skills", "Author", "Location"]
        }
    ],
    "follow_up_actions": [
        {
            "action": "implement_proper_rate_limiting",
            "estimated_time": "30_minutes",
            "models_benefited": ["User", "JobPost"]
        },
        {
            "action": "optimize_database_indexes",
            "estimated_time": "60_minutes",
            "models_benefited": ["JobPost", "User"]
        }
    ]
}

# Print investigation analysis
print("Django Production Debugging Investigation - anihpj Critical Incident:")
print("=" * 68)
print(f"Incident ID: {investigation_context['incident_id']}")
print(f"Severity: {investigation_context['severity'].upper()}")
print(f"Investigation Start: {investigation_context['investigation_start']}")

print(f"\n📁 File Extraction Summary:")
for category, details in extraction_plan.items():
    category_name = category.replace('_', ' ').title()
    priority = details['priority'].title()
    source_count = len(details['source_paths'])
    
    print(f"\n   📂 {category_name}:")
    print(f"      Priority: {priority}")
    print(f"      Files/Paths: {source_count}")
    
    if 'models_relevance' in details:
        print(f"      Model Relevance: High")
    elif 'analysis_focus' in details:
        print(f"      Analysis Focus: Database Performance")

print(f"\n🔍 Extracted File Analysis:")
django_analysis = extracted_files_analysis['django_log_analysis']
print(f"   Django Log: {django_analysis['file_size']}, {django_analysis['lines_count']:,} lines")
print(f"   Errors: {django_analysis['error_entries']:,}, Warnings: {django_analysis['warning_entries']:,}")

print(f"\n📊 Model-Specific Error Analysis:")
model_errors = django_analysis['model_error_breakdown']
for model, errors in model_errors.items():
    model_name = model.replace('_model_errors', '').replace('_', ' ').title()
    total_errors = sum(errors[key] for key in errors.keys() if isinstance(errors[key], int))
    
    print(f"\n   🔴 {model_name} Model ({total_errors} errors):")
    for error_type, count in errors.items():
        if isinstance(count, int):
            error_name = error_type.replace('_', ' ').title()
            print(f"      - {error_name}: {count}")
    
    if 'critical_patterns' in errors:
        critical_pattern = errors['critical_patterns'][0]
        print(f"      Critical: {critical_pattern}")

print(f"\n💾 Database Performance Issues:")
db_analysis = extracted_files_analysis['database_log_analysis']
print(f"   Log Size: {db_analysis['postgresql_log_size']}")
print(f"   Slow Queries: {db_analysis['slow_queries']}")
print(f"   Deadlocks: {db_analysis['deadlocks']}")

db_perf = db_analysis['model_performance_issues']
for table, issues in db_perf.items():
    table_name = table.replace('_table', '').replace('_', ' ').title()
    slow_queries = issues.get('slow_queries', 0)
    deadlocks = issues.get('deadlocks', 0)
    
    print(f"   📊 {table_name}: {slow_queries} slow queries, {deadlocks} deadlocks")

print(f"\n🎯 Root Cause Analysis:")
primary = root_cause_analysis['primary_causes']
print(f"   Primary Causes: {len(primary)}")

for i, cause in enumerate(primary, 1):
    cause_name = cause['cause'].replace('_', ' ').title()
    severity = cause['severity'].title()
    models = ', '.join(cause['models_impacted'])
    
    print(f"\n   {i}. {cause_name} ({severity}):")
    print(f"      Models Impacted: {models}")
    print(f"      Impact: {cause['impact_description'].replace('_', ' ').title()}")
    print(f"      Priority: {cause['fix_priority'].title()}")

print(f"\n🔧 Remediation Plan:")
immediate = remediation_plan['immediate_actions']
print(f"   Immediate Actions: {len(immediate)}")

for action in immediate:
    action_name = action['action'].replace('_', ' ').title()
    time_est = action['estimated_time'].replace('_', ' ')
    models = ', '.join(action['models_benefited'])
    
    print(f"\n   ⚡ {action_name}:")
    print(f"      Time: {time_est}")
    print(f"      Models Benefited: {models}")
    print(f"      Steps: {len(action['steps'])}")

print(f"\n✅ Investigation Summary:")
print(f"   🔍 Complete file extraction and analysis performed")
print(f"   📊 All 5 Django models error patterns identified")
print(f"   🎯 3 critical root causes discovered")
print(f"   🔧 Immediate remediation plan established")
print(f"   ⚡ Estimated resolution time: 35 minutes")
print(f"   💾 Evidence preserved for post-incident review")

print(f"\n📋 Next Steps:")
print(f"   1. Execute immediate remediation actions")
print(f"   2. Monitor system recovery metrics")
print(f"   3. Implement preventive measures")
print(f"   4. Conduct post-incident review")
print(f"   5. Update monitoring and alerting systems")

Debug Mode In Production (Critical): Models Impacted: User, JobPost, Skills, Author, Location Impact: Performance Degradation And Security Exposure Priority: Immediate 2. Elasticsearch Cluster Failure (High): Models Impacted: JobPost, Skills Impact: Search Functionality Complete Failure Priority: Urgent 3. Database Connection Pool Exhaustion (High): Models Impacted: User, JobPost, Skills, Author, Location Impact: Intermittent Service Unavailability Priority: Urgent 🔧 Remediation Plan: Immediate Actions: 3 ⚡ Disable Debug Mode In Production: Time: 5 minutes Models Benefited: User, JobPost, Skills, Author, Location Steps: 3 ⚡ Restart Elasticsearch Cluster: Time: 20 minutes Models Benefited: JobPost, Skills Steps: 3 ⚡ Increase Database Connection Pool: Time: 10 minutes Models Benefited: User, JobPost, Skills, Author, Location Steps: 3 ✅ Investigation Summary: 🔍 Complete file extraction and analysis performed 📊 All 5 Django models error patterns identified 🎯 3 critical root causes discovered 🔧 Immediate remediation plan established ⚡ Estimated resolution time: 35 minutes 💾 Evidence preserved for post-incident review

docker compose push

Official Docs: docker compose push | Docker CLI: docker compose push

Push service images to a Docker registry.

Full Description

The docker compose push command pushes service images to a Docker registry. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables you to upload built Django application images, database images with custom configurations, and supporting service images to container registries for deployment, sharing across environments, or backup purposes.

When pushing your anihpj Django application images, the command uploads all defined services that have been built locally to configured registries. This enables deployment to production environments, sharing with team members, and maintaining versioned releases of your Django application stack including web servers, background workers, and custom database configurations with your jobpost app models and data.

Syntax

# Push all service images
docker compose push

# Push specific service
docker compose push [SERVICE]

# Push Django web service
docker compose push web

# Push with ignore-push-failures
docker compose push --ignore-push-failures

Parameters Description

  • SERVICE: Optional service name to push specific service image. Use for selective Django component deployment like 'web', 'worker', 'db' for targeted registry uploads.
  • --ignore-push-failures: Continue pushing other images even if some fail. Useful when pushing multiple Django services with different registry access permissions.

Attributes

  • Registry URL: Target container registry for image uploads. Configure in compose file or Docker daemon settings for Django application distribution.
  • Image Tags: Version tags attached to pushed images. Use semantic versioning for Django application releases and environment tracking.
  • Layer Count: Number of image layers uploaded during push operation. Optimize Django Dockerfile layer structure for faster pushes.
  • Push Status: Success/failure status for each service image push. Monitor for deployment pipeline validation and error handling.
  • Compression Ratio: Size reduction achieved during image layer compression. Affects Django application deployment speed and registry storage.

Methods

Selective Service Push

Syntax: docker compose push [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Specific Django service names to push to registry

Push only specified Django services to registry, enabling targeted deployments and selective component updates without full stack re-upload.

Fault-Tolerant Push

Syntax: docker compose push --ignore-push-failures

Parameters: None

Continue pushing Django services even if individual service pushes fail, ensuring maximum deployment coverage for multi-service Django applications.

Return Value

Returns push status and progress for each service image. Shows upload progress, layer information, and any authentication or network errors encountered during Django image registry upload.

  • Success (0): All service images pushed successfully
  • Error (1): Push failure due to authentication or network issues
  • Error (125): Invalid service names or compose configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Production Django Stack Push (Complete Application Deployment)

Push complete Django application stack to production registry for deployment across multiple environments with version control.

# Tag images for production registry
docker compose build
docker tag anihpj_web:latest registry.company.com/anihpj/web:v2.1.0
docker tag anihpj_worker:latest registry.company.com/anihpj/worker:v2.1.0

# Push all Django services to production registry
docker compose push

Django Models Context & Deployment Strategy:

# Django models being deployed in production push
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100, db_index=True)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    level = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    demand_score = models.FloatField(default=0.0)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200, db_index=True)
    required_skills = models.ManyToManyField(Skills)
    salary_min = models.IntegerField()
    salary_max = models.IntegerField()
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
# Production deployment targets for registry push
deployment_environments = {
    'registry.company.com/anihpj/web:v2.1.0': {
        'models': ['User', 'Author', 'Location', 'JobPost', 'Skills'],
        'features': ['authentication', 'job_search', 'skills_verification'],
        'environment': 'production'
    },
    'registry.company.com/anihpj/worker:v2.1.0': {
        'models': ['JobPost', 'Skills'],
        'features': ['background_processing', 'skills_analysis'],
        'environment': 'production'
    }
}
The push refers to repository [registry.company.com/anihpj/web] v2.1.0: digest: sha256:a1b2c3d4... size: 2847 The push refers to repository [registry.company.com/anihpj/worker] v2.1.0: digest: sha256:e5f6g7h8... size: 1923

docker compose create

Official Docs: docker compose create | Docker CLI: docker compose create

Create containers for all services without starting them.

Full Description

The docker compose create command creates containers for all services defined in the compose file without starting them. For your anihpj Django project, this command prepares all Django application containers, databases, and supporting services in a ready state for later execution, enabling staged deployment workflows and container inspection before startup.

When creating containers for your anihpj Django application, the command allocates system resources, establishes networking configurations, mounts volumes for data persistence, and prepares all environment variables without actually running the services. This allows for pre-deployment validation, container configuration inspection, and controlled startup sequences for your jobpost app components.

Syntax

# Create all service containers
docker compose create

# Create specific service containers  
docker compose create [SERVICE...]

# Create Django web service container
docker compose create web

# Create with forced recreation
docker compose create --force-recreate

Parameters Description

  • SERVICE: Optional service names to create specific containers. Target individual Django components like 'web', 'db', 'worker' for selective container preparation.
  • --force-recreate: Recreate containers even if configuration hasn't changed. Useful for Django development when testing container setup modifications.
  • --no-recreate: Don't recreate existing containers. Optimize Django deployment by reusing existing container configurations when possible.

Attributes

  • Container Status: Created containers remain in 'Created' state until started. Enable controlled Django application initialization sequences.
  • Resource Allocation: Memory and CPU limits applied during container creation. Configure appropriate resources for Django application performance requirements.
  • Network Configuration: Container networking established but inactive until startup. Prepare Django service communication without active connections.
  • Volume Mounting: Data volumes attached to containers ready for use. Ensure Django database persistence and media file access preparation.
  • Environment Setup: All environment variables loaded and ready for service execution. Prepare Django settings and configuration values for runtime.

Methods

Selective Container Creation

Syntax: docker compose create [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Specific Django service names to create containers for

Create containers for specified Django services only, enabling targeted preparation and staged deployment workflows for complex application stacks.

Forced Recreation

Syntax: docker compose create --force-recreate [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Optional service names to recreate

Force recreation of Django service containers regardless of existing state, ensuring clean container environments for debugging and testing.

Return Value

Returns container creation status and container IDs for successfully created services. Shows any configuration errors or resource allocation failures for Django application services.

  • Success (0): All specified containers created successfully
  • Error (1): Container creation failure due to resource or configuration issues
  • Error (125): Invalid compose file or service specification

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Staged Django Deployment (Pre-Start Container Preparation)

Create Django application containers for staged deployment workflow with validation before startup for production readiness testing.

# Create all Django service containers without starting
docker compose create

# Inspect created containers
docker compose ps -a

# Validate container configurations
docker inspect anihpj_web_1 | grep -E "(Image|Env|Mounts)"

Django Models Context & Staging Strategy:

# Django models prepared for staged deployment
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    date_joined = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
class Author(models.Model):
    user = models.OneToOneField(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    bio = models.TextField()
    expertise_level = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    portfolio_url = models.URLField(blank=True)
    
# Staged deployment configuration for container creation
staging_config = {
    'web_container': {
        'models_ready': ['User', 'Author', 'JobPost', 'Skills', 'Location'],
        'environment': 'staging',
        'health_checks': ['database_connection', 'redis_connection'],
        'pre_start_validation': True
    },
    'db_container': {
        'data_persistence': True,
        'backup_mounted': True,
        'migration_ready': True
    }
}

# Container creation readiness checklist
print("Django Container Creation Readiness:")
print(f"Models prepared: {len(staging_config['web_container']['models_ready'])}")
print(f"Health checks configured: {len(staging_config['web_container']['health_checks'])}")
print(f"Pre-start validation: {staging_config['web_container']['pre_start_validation']}")
Creating anihpj_db_1 ... done Creating anihpj_redis_1 ... done Creating anihpj_web_1 ... done Creating anihpj_worker_1 ... done Container Status: NAME IMAGE STATUS anihpj_db_1 postgres:14 Created anihpj_redis_1 redis:6-alpine Created anihpj_web_1 anihpj_web:latest Created anihpj_worker_1 anihpj_worker:latest Created

docker compose attach

Official Docs: docker compose attach | Docker CLI: docker compose attach

Attach to a running service container for interactive debugging.

Full Description

The docker compose attach command attaches your terminal to a running service container, providing direct access to the container's output streams. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables real-time monitoring of Django application logs, debugging running processes, and interactive troubleshooting of web servers, background workers, and database operations.

When attaching to your anihpj Django application containers, you gain direct access to stdout, stderr, and stdin streams, enabling live log monitoring, interactive debugging sessions, and real-time analysis of your jobpost app performance, user authentication flows, and background task processing.

Syntax

# Attach to service container
docker compose attach [SERVICE]

# Attach to Django web service
docker compose attach web

# Attach with no-stdin
docker compose attach --no-stdin web

# Attach and detach with Ctrl+C
docker compose attach --detach-keys="ctrl-c" web

Parameters Description

  • SERVICE: Name of the running service to attach to. Target specific Django components like 'web', 'worker', 'db' for focused debugging sessions.
  • --no-stdin: Don't attach stdin. Use for read-only log monitoring of Django services without interactive input capability.
  • --detach-keys: Specify key sequence to detach from container. Configure custom detach commands for Django debugging workflows.

Attributes

  • Stream Access: Direct connection to container's stdout and stderr streams. Monitor Django application logs, error messages, and debug output in real-time.
  • Interactive Mode: Terminal input forwarded to attached container. Enable interactive Django management commands and debugging sessions.
  • Detach Sequence: Key combination to exit attachment without stopping container. Maintain Django service continuity during debugging sessions.
  • Signal Handling: Container signals handled through attachment. Monitor Django application health and process lifecycle events.
  • Process Monitoring: Real-time visibility into container process behavior. Observe Django request handling, task processing, and system resource usage.

Methods

Read-Only Monitoring

Syntax: docker compose attach --no-stdin [SERVICE]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Django service name to monitor without interaction

Monitor Django service output without ability to send input, perfect for safe production log monitoring and debugging observation.

Interactive Debugging

Syntax: docker compose attach [SERVICE]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Django service name for interactive session

Establish full interactive connection to Django service container for debugging, management commands, and real-time troubleshooting.

Return Value

Returns real-time output from attached service container. Shows Django application logs, error messages, and any interactive session input/output until detachment.

  • Attached: Successfully connected to running service container
  • Error (1): Service not running or container access denied
  • Error (125): Invalid service name or compose configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Live Django Debug Session (Real-time Error Monitoring)

Attach to running Django web service to monitor live error logs and debug authentication issues in real-time.

# Attach to Django web service for live debugging
docker compose attach web

# In another terminal, trigger test requests
curl http://localhost:8000/api/auth/login/
curl http://localhost:8000/api/jobs/search/

Django Models Context & Debug Monitoring:

# Django models being debugged through attach session
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    last_login = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)
    failed_login_attempts = models.IntegerField(default=0)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    
    def authenticate(self, password):
        # Debug point for live monitoring
        print(f"DEBUG: Authentication attempt for {self.username}")
        return check_password(password, self.password)

class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    author = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    view_count = models.IntegerField(default=0)
    
    def increment_views(self):
        # Monitor view tracking in real-time
        print(f"DEBUG: JobPost {self.id} view increment: {self.view_count + 1}")
        self.view_count += 1
        self.save()

# Real-time debugging output visible through attach
debug_events = [
    "User john_doe authentication attempt",
    "JobPost 'Senior Developer' view increment: 45",
    "Skills verification for 'Python' - Level: Expert",
    "Location search query for 'New York' - Results: 234"
]
[2026-01-12 14:23:45] INFO: Django application starting [2026-01-12 14:23:46] DEBUG: Authentication attempt for john_doe [2026-01-12 14:23:46] INFO: User john_doe logged in successfully [2026-01-12 14:23:48] DEBUG: JobPost 15 view increment: 45 [2026-01-12 14:23:48] INFO: JobPost search executed - Query: 'Python Developer' [2026-01-12 14:23:49] DEBUG: Skills verification for 'Python' - Level: Expert [2026-01-12 14:23:50] ERROR: Location service timeout - Query: 'New York' [2026-01-12 14:23:51] WARNING: Redis connection retry attempt 1/3

docker compose volumes

Official Docs: docker compose volumes | Docker CLI: docker compose volumes

Manage Docker volumes for compose services.

Full Description

The docker compose volumes command manages Docker volumes associated with compose services. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables you to list, inspect, and manage persistent storage volumes for database data, media files, static assets, and application logs across your Django application stack.

When managing volumes for your anihpj Django application, this command helps maintain data persistence for PostgreSQL databases, Redis cache storage, user-uploaded media files, and log retention across container restarts and deployments. This ensures your jobpost app data, user profiles, and application state remain persistent and recoverable.

Syntax

# List all project volumes
docker compose volumes ls

# Remove all project volumes  
docker compose volumes rm

# Remove specific volumes
docker compose volumes rm [VOLUME...]

# Remove with force (no confirmation)
docker compose volumes rm -f [VOLUME...]

Parameters Description

  • VOLUME: Specific volume names to remove. Target individual Django data volumes like database storage, media files, or log directories.
  • -f, --force: Remove volumes without confirmation prompt. Use with caution for Django production data volumes containing critical application data.
  • ls: List all volumes associated with the compose project. View Django application data storage usage and volume configuration.

Attributes

  • Volume Names: Unique identifiers for project volumes. Track Django database storage, media uploads, and log persistence volumes.
  • Driver Types: Storage driver used for volume creation. Configure appropriate storage backends for Django application data requirements.
  • Mount Points: Container paths where volumes are mounted. Map Django application directories to persistent storage locations.
  • Data Size: Current storage usage for each volume. Monitor Django application data growth and storage capacity planning.
  • Creation Time: When volumes were first created. Track Django application data lifecycle and backup scheduling requirements.

Methods

Volume Listing

Syntax: docker compose volumes ls

Parameters: None

Display all volumes associated with Django compose project, enabling data storage monitoring and volume usage analysis for application management.

Selective Removal

Syntax: docker compose volumes rm [VOLUME...]

Parameters: VOLUME (string) - Specific volume names to remove from Django project

Remove specific Django data volumes while preserving others, enabling targeted cleanup and data management for development and testing workflows.

Return Value

Returns volume management operation results including volume names, removal status, and any data preservation warnings for Django application volumes.

  • Success (0): Volume operation completed successfully
  • Error (1): Volume in use or permission denied
  • Error (125): Invalid volume names or project configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Django Data Volume Audit (Storage Management)

List and analyze all Django project volumes to understand data storage usage and plan capacity management for production deployment.

# List all Django project volumes
docker compose volumes ls

# Get detailed volume information
docker volume inspect anihpj_postgres_data anihpj_media_files anihpj_logs

# Check volume disk usage
docker system df -v | grep anihpj

Django Models Context & Volume Strategy:

# Django models with volume storage requirements
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    profile_image = models.ImageField(upload_to='profiles/', blank=True)  # Stored in media volume
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    company_logo = models.ImageField(upload_to='company_logos/', blank=True)  # Media volume
    attachments = models.FileField(upload_to='job_attachments/', blank=True)  # Media volume
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)  # Database volume
    
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    icon = models.ImageField(upload_to='skill_icons/', blank=True)  # Media volume
    verification_docs = models.FileField(upload_to='verifications/', blank=True)  # Media volume

# Volume storage mapping for Django application
volume_mapping = {
    'anihpj_postgres_data': {
        'purpose': 'Database storage',
        'models': ['User', 'JobPost', 'Skills', 'Author', 'Location'],
        'critical': True,
        'backup_frequency': 'daily'
    },
    'anihpj_media_files': {
        'purpose': 'User uploaded files',
        'fields': ['profile_image', 'company_logo', 'attachments', 'skill_icons'],
        'size_estimate': '15GB',
        'backup_frequency': 'weekly'
    },
    'anihpj_static_files': {
        'purpose': 'Django static assets',
        'content': ['CSS', 'JavaScript', 'Images'],
        'rebuild_capable': True
    }
}

# Print volume analysis
print("Django Volume Storage Analysis:")
for volume, config in volume_mapping.items():
    print(f"Volume: {volume}")
    print(f"  Purpose: {config['purpose']}")
    print(f"  Critical: {config.get('critical', False)}")
    if 'size_estimate' in config:
        print(f"  Estimated Size: {config['size_estimate']}")
VOLUME NAME DRIVER SCOPE anihpj_postgres_data local local anihpj_redis_data local local anihpj_media_files local local anihpj_static_files local local anihpj_logs local local Volume Details: - anihpj_postgres_data: 2.3GB (Database tables, indexes) - anihpj_media_files: 856MB (User uploads, company logos) - anihpj_static_files: 45MB (CSS, JS, static images) - anihpj_logs: 234MB (Application and access logs) - anihpj_redis_data: 78MB (Cache and session data)

docker compose wait

Official Docs: docker compose wait | Docker CLI: docker compose wait

Wait for services to reach a specific condition or state.

Full Description

The docker compose wait command waits for services to reach specific states or conditions. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables synchronized startup sequences, dependency management, and automated deployment workflows by waiting for database initialization, service health checks, and application readiness before proceeding with dependent operations.

When orchestrating your anihpj Django application startup, this command ensures proper initialization order by waiting for PostgreSQL databases to accept connections, Redis caches to be ready, and Django migration completion before starting web servers or background workers, preventing race conditions in your jobpost app deployment.

Syntax

# Wait for service to stop
docker compose wait [SERVICE...]

# Wait for multiple Django services
docker compose wait web db worker

# Wait with timeout
docker compose wait --timeout 60 db

Parameters Description

  • SERVICE: Service names to wait for completion. Specify Django components like 'db', 'web', 'worker' to ensure proper startup sequencing.
  • --timeout: Maximum time to wait in seconds. Prevent infinite waits for Django service startup and deployment automation workflows.

Attributes

  • Wait State: Condition being waited for (typically service completion). Monitor Django service lifecycle for deployment coordination.
  • Timeout Duration: Maximum wait time before operation fails. Configure appropriate timeouts for Django application startup patterns.
  • Service Status: Current state of services being monitored. Track Django component readiness during orchestrated deployments.
  • Exit Conditions: Events that trigger wait completion. Define Django application readiness criteria for automated workflows.
  • Error Handling: Timeout and failure behavior configuration. Implement robust Django deployment error handling and recovery.

Methods

Multi-Service Wait

Syntax: docker compose wait [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Multiple Django service names to wait for simultaneously

Wait for multiple Django services to complete, enabling coordinated shutdown and maintenance workflows for complex application stacks.

Timeout Management

Syntax: docker compose wait --timeout [SECONDS] [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SECONDS (integer) - Maximum wait duration, SERVICE (string) - Django services to monitor

Apply timeout constraints to Django service wait operations, preventing deployment pipeline stalls and enabling automated failure handling.

Return Value

Returns when specified services reach target state or timeout occurs. Shows service completion status and any timeout or error conditions for Django application coordination.

  • Success (0): All specified services reached target state
  • Error (1): Timeout reached before services completed
  • Error (125): Invalid service names or configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Coordinated Django Maintenance (Service Synchronization)

Wait for Django services to complete maintenance operations before proceeding with deployment updates and system configuration changes.

# Start maintenance mode
docker compose stop web worker

# Wait for services to fully stop
docker compose wait web worker

# Proceed with maintenance tasks
echo "Services stopped, starting maintenance..."

Django Models Context & Maintenance Coordination:

# Django models requiring coordinated maintenance
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    search_vector = models.TextField()  # Requires search index rebuild
    last_updated = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100) 
    demand_score = models.FloatField()  # Requires batch recalculation
    verification_status = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    
# Maintenance operations requiring service coordination
maintenance_tasks = {
    'search_index_rebuild': {
        'affected_models': ['JobPost'],
        'duration_estimate': '15 minutes',
        'requires_services_down': ['web', 'worker']
    },
    'skills_demand_recalculation': {
        'affected_models': ['Skills', 'JobPost'],
        'duration_estimate': '25 minutes', 
        'requires_services_down': ['worker']
    },
    'database_optimization': {
        'affected_models': ['User', 'JobPost', 'Skills', 'Author', 'Location'],
        'duration_estimate': '45 minutes',
        'requires_services_down': ['web', 'worker', 'db']
    }
}

# Maintenance coordination workflow
print("Django Maintenance Coordination:")
for task, config in maintenance_tasks.items():
    print(f"Task: {task}")
    print(f"  Affected Models: {', '.join(config['affected_models'])}")
    print(f"  Duration: {config['duration_estimate']}")
    print(f"  Services Down: {', '.join(config['requires_services_down'])}")
Stopping anihpj_web_1 ... done Stopping anihpj_worker_1 ... done Waiting for services to complete... anihpj_web_1 stopped anihpj_worker_1 stopped Services stopped, starting maintenance... ✅ Ready for maintenance operations

docker compose watch

Official Docs: docker compose watch | Docker CLI: docker compose watch

Watch for changes in compose services and automatically reload.

Full Description

The docker compose watch command monitors your Django application files for changes and automatically reloads services when modifications are detected. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables rapid development workflows by automatically restarting Django servers, rebuilding images, and synchronizing code changes without manual intervention.

When developing your anihpj Django application, watch mode detects changes to Python files, templates, static assets, and configuration files, automatically triggering Django server reloads, template recompilation, and service restarts to keep your development environment synchronized with your jobpost app code changes.

Syntax

# Start watch mode for all services
docker compose watch

# Watch specific services
docker compose watch [SERVICE...]

# Watch Django web service only
docker compose watch web

# Watch with verbose output
docker compose watch --verbose web

Parameters Description

  • SERVICE: Optional service names to watch for changes. Target specific Django components like 'web', 'worker' for focused development workflows.
  • --verbose: Enable detailed logging of file change detection. Monitor Django application reload behavior and debugging development issues.
  • --no-up: Don't start services, only watch running ones. Monitor existing Django development containers without initialization.

Attributes

  • File Monitoring: Real-time detection of changes in watched directories. Track Django source code, templates, and static file modifications.
  • Reload Triggers: Events that cause service restarts or rebuilds. Configure Django development workflow automation based on file change patterns.
  • Watch Patterns: File extensions and paths monitored for changes. Customize Django application file watching for optimal development experience.
  • Reload Speed: Time between change detection and service reload. Optimize Django development feedback loops for rapid iteration.
  • Change History: Log of detected changes and triggered reloads. Debug Django development workflow and change detection behavior.

Methods

Selective Service Watch

Syntax: docker compose watch [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Specific Django service names to monitor for changes

Monitor specific Django services for file changes, enabling focused development workflows and optimized resource usage during coding sessions.

Verbose Monitoring

Syntax: docker compose watch --verbose [SERVICE...]

Parameters: SERVICE (string) - Django services to watch with detailed logging

Enable detailed logging of Django file change detection and reload behavior for debugging development workflow issues and optimization.

Return Value

Returns continuous monitoring status and file change notifications. Shows Django application reload events, file modification details, and any service restart errors during development.

  • Monitoring: Continuous file watching active
  • Error (1): Watch configuration error or service access issues
  • Error (125): Invalid service specification or compose file

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Django Development Hot Reload (Rapid Development Workflow)

Enable automatic Django application reloading during development for instant feedback on code changes across all models and views.

# Start Django development with hot reload
docker compose watch web

# In another terminal, modify Django files
echo "# Development changes" >> jobpost/models.py
echo "print('Debug: Model updated')" >> jobpost/views.py

Django Models Context & Development Workflow:

# Django models being developed with hot reload
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100, db_index=True)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    level = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    demand_score = models.FloatField(default=0.0)
    # New field being added during development
    trend_direction = models.CharField(max_length=10, default='stable')  # Hot reload trigger
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def calculate_trend(self):
        # Method being developed with live updates
        print(f"DEBUG: Calculating trend for {self.name}")  # Watch reload trigger
        return self.trend_direction
    
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200, db_index=True)
    required_skills = models.ManyToManyField(Skills)
    # New development features
    ai_match_score = models.FloatField(default=0.0)  # Hot reload on save
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def update_ai_score(self):
        # Live development method
        print(f"DEBUG: AI score update for {self.title}")  # Triggers reload
        self.ai_match_score = calculate_ai_score(self.required_skills.all())
        self.save()

# Development watch configuration for Django files
watch_patterns = {
    'python_files': ['**/*.py'],
    'template_files': ['**/templates/**/*.html'],
    'static_files': ['**/static/**/*.css', '**/static/**/*.js'],
    'config_files': ['settings.py', 'urls.py', 'wsgi.py']
}

# File change events during development
development_changes = [
    {'file': 'jobpost/models.py', 'change': 'Added trend_direction field', 'reload_time': '2.3s'},
    {'file': 'jobpost/views.py', 'change': 'Updated JobPostViewSet', 'reload_time': '1.8s'},
    {'file': 'templates/job_detail.html', 'change': 'Added AI score display', 'reload_time': '1.2s'}
]

print("Django Development Watch Events:")
for change in development_changes:
    print(f"File: {change['file']}")
    print(f"Change: {change['change']}")
    print(f"Reload Time: {change['reload_time']}")
📁 Watching for file changes in Django project... [14:23:45] File changed: jobpost/models.py [14:23:47] 🔄 Reloading Django web service... [14:23:49] ✅ Django server restarted successfully [14:24:12] File changed: jobpost/views.py [14:24:13] 🔄 Reloading Django web service... [14:24:15] ✅ Django server restarted successfully [14:24:34] File changed: templates/job_detail.html [14:24:35] 🔄 Reloading Django web service... [14:24:36] ✅ Template cache cleared, server ready 💡 Hot reload active - Django development accelerated

docker compose alpha

Official Docs: docker compose alpha | Docker CLI: docker compose alpha

Experimental Docker Compose features for advanced functionality.

Full Description

The docker compose alpha command provides access to experimental Docker Compose features in development. For your anihpj Django project, these alpha features enable advanced deployment testing, service visualization, and scaling experiments that may become standard features in future Docker Compose releases.

When experimenting with your anihpj Django application deployment, alpha commands provide early access to enhanced functionality for testing deployment strategies, visualizing service dependencies, and experimenting with scaling patterns before they become part of the stable Docker Compose feature set.

Syntax

# View alpha subcommands
docker compose alpha --help

# Alpha dry-run functionality
docker compose alpha dry-run

# Alpha scaling features
docker compose alpha scale

# Alpha visualization
docker compose alpha viz

Parameters Description

  • dry-run: Preview compose operations without execution. Test Django deployment configurations safely before applying changes to running services.
  • scale: Advanced scaling functionality. Experiment with Django service scaling patterns and load distribution strategies.
  • viz: Service visualization and dependency mapping. Generate Django application architecture diagrams and service relationship maps.

Attributes

  • Experimental Status: Features under development and testing. Use alpha commands for Django development and testing environments only.
  • API Stability: Command syntax may change between Docker versions. Document Django deployment scripts using alpha features for maintenance.
  • Feature Preview: Early access to upcoming functionality. Test future Django deployment capabilities and provide feedback to Docker.
  • Compatibility: May require specific Docker Compose versions. Verify Django development environment compatibility before using alpha features.
  • Documentation: Limited official documentation available. Use alpha commands for Django experimentation and proof-of-concept work.

Methods

Preview Operations

Syntax: docker compose alpha dry-run [COMMAND]

Parameters: COMMAND (string) - Compose command to preview without execution

Preview Django deployment operations without actually executing them, enabling safe testing of configuration changes and deployment strategies.

Advanced Visualization

Syntax: docker compose alpha viz [OPTIONS]

Parameters: OPTIONS - Visualization configuration options

Generate visual representations of Django application architecture and service dependencies for documentation and analysis purposes.

Return Value

Returns alpha feature output based on specific subcommand used. Shows experimental functionality results for Django application testing and development workflows.

  • Success (0): Alpha feature executed successfully
  • Error (1): Alpha feature error or unsupported functionality
  • Error (125): Invalid alpha command or configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Django Deployment Preview (Safe Configuration Testing)

Use alpha dry-run to preview Django deployment changes without executing them, ensuring safe configuration testing for production environments.

# Preview Django deployment without execution
docker compose alpha dry-run up

# Preview scaling changes
docker compose alpha dry-run up --scale web=3

# Preview down operation
docker compose alpha dry-run down

Django Models Context & Preview Strategy:

# Django models affected by deployment preview
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    # Impact: Authentication service scaling preview
    
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    author = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    is_published = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    # Impact: Job processing service deployment preview
    
# Deployment preview configuration for Django services
preview_config = {
    'web_service': {
        'current_replicas': 1,
        'target_replicas': 3,
        'models_affected': ['User', 'JobPost', 'Skills'],
        'preview_changes': [
            'Load balancer configuration',
            'Database connection pooling',
            'Session affinity setup'
        ]
    },
    'worker_service': {
        'current_replicas': 2,
        'target_replicas': 4,
        'models_affected': ['JobPost', 'Skills'],
        'preview_changes': [
            'Task queue scaling',
            'Background job distribution',
            'Resource allocation'
        ]
    }
}

# Preview operation analysis
print("Django Deployment Preview Analysis:")
for service, config in preview_config.items():
    print(f"Service: {service}")
    print(f"  Scaling: {config['current_replicas']} → {config['target_replicas']}")
    print(f"  Models: {', '.join(config['models_affected'])}")
    print(f"  Changes: {len(config['preview_changes'])} configuration updates")
🔍 Django Deployment Preview (Dry Run): Service Changes: web: Action: scale Current: 1 replica Target: 3 replicas Image: anihpj_web:latest Resources: CPU 0.5, Memory 512MB per replica worker: Action: scale Current: 2 replicas Target: 4 replicas Image: anihpj_worker:latest Resources: CPU 0.3, Memory 256MB per replica Network Changes: Load balancer: Configure for 3 web replicas Service discovery: Update for 4 worker replicas 💡 Preview completed - No services modified

docker compose alpha dry-run

Official Docs: docker compose alpha dry-run | Docker CLI: docker compose alpha dry-run

Preview compose operations without executing them.

Full Description

The docker compose alpha dry-run command previews compose operations without actually executing them. For your anihpj Django project, this experimental feature enables safe testing of deployment configurations, scaling operations, and service modifications before applying changes to your Django application stack.

Syntax

# Dry run any compose command
docker compose alpha dry-run [COMMAND] [OPTIONS]

# Preview up operation
docker compose alpha dry-run up

# Preview scaling
docker compose alpha dry-run up --scale web=3

Return Value

Shows preview of operations that would be performed without executing them. Displays Django service changes, resource modifications, and configuration updates for safe testing.

docker compose alpha scale

Official Docs: docker compose alpha scale | Docker CLI: docker compose alpha scale

Advanced scaling functionality for compose services.

Full Description

The docker compose alpha scale command provides enhanced scaling capabilities for compose services. For your anihpj Django project, this experimental feature enables advanced scaling patterns and load distribution strategies for Django web servers and background workers.

Syntax

# Advanced scaling with alpha features
docker compose alpha scale [SERVICE=REPLICAS...]

# Scale Django web service
docker compose alpha scale web=3

Return Value

Returns enhanced scaling operation results with advanced metrics and load distribution information for Django application services.

docker compose alpha viz

Official Docs: docker compose alpha viz | Docker CLI: docker compose alpha viz

Visualize service dependencies and architecture.

Full Description

The docker compose alpha viz command generates visual representations of service dependencies and application architecture. For your anihpj Django project, this experimental feature creates diagrams showing relationships between Django services, databases, and supporting infrastructure.

Syntax

# Generate service visualization
docker compose alpha viz

# Output to specific format
docker compose alpha viz --format=dot

Return Value

Returns visual representation data for Django application architecture and service dependency mapping in various output formats.

docker compose bridge

Official Docs: docker compose bridge | Docker CLI: docker compose bridge

Bridge functionality for migrating from legacy Docker Compose formats.

Full Description

The docker compose bridge command provides migration and compatibility functionality for legacy Docker Compose configurations. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables smooth transitions from older compose file formats and provides transformation utilities for modernizing Django deployment configurations.

Syntax

# Bridge operations
docker compose bridge [COMMAND]

# Convert legacy configurations  
docker compose bridge convert

# Manage transformations
docker compose bridge transformations

Return Value

Returns bridge operation results including conversion status and transformation details for Django compose file migrations.

docker compose bridge convert

Official Docs: docker compose bridge convert | Docker CLI: docker compose bridge convert

Convert legacy compose configurations to modern format.

Full Description

The docker compose bridge convert command converts legacy Docker Compose configurations to modern formats. For your anihpj Django project, this enables migration from older compose file versions to current standards with updated syntax and features.

Syntax

# Convert compose file format
docker compose bridge convert [OPTIONS]

# Convert with output file
docker compose bridge convert -o docker-compose-new.yml

Return Value

Returns converted compose configuration in modern format for Django application deployment with updated syntax and features.

docker compose bridge transformations

Official Docs: docker compose bridge transformations | Docker CLI: docker compose bridge transformations

Manage configuration transformation rules and templates.

Full Description

The docker compose bridge transformations command manages configuration transformation rules. For your anihpj Django project, this enables custom migration patterns and transformation templates for complex Django deployment configurations.

Syntax

# Manage transformations
docker compose bridge transformations [COMMAND]

# Create transformation rules
docker compose bridge transformations create

# List available transformations  
docker compose bridge transformations list

Return Value

Returns transformation management results for Django compose configuration migration workflows.

docker compose bridge transformations create

Official Docs: docker compose bridge transformations create | Docker CLI: docker compose bridge transformations create

Create custom transformation rules for compose file migration.

Full Description

The docker compose bridge transformations create command creates custom transformation rules for compose file migration. For your anihpj Django project, this enables creation of specialized migration patterns for complex Django deployment configurations.

Syntax

# Create transformation rule
docker compose bridge transformations create [OPTIONS]

Return Value

Returns creation status for custom Django compose transformation rules and templates.

docker compose bridge transformations list

Official Docs: docker compose bridge transformations list | Docker CLI: docker compose bridge transformations list

List available transformation rules and templates.

Full Description

The docker compose bridge transformations list command lists available transformation rules and templates. For your anihpj Django project, this shows available migration patterns for Django deployment configuration updates.

Syntax

# List transformations
docker compose bridge transformations list

Return Value

Returns list of available Django compose transformation rules and migration templates.

docker compose publish

Official Docs: docker compose publish | Docker CLI: docker compose publish

Publish compose application to Docker Hub or registry.

Full Description

The docker compose publish command publishes complete compose applications to registries. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables packaging and distribution of entire Django application stacks including all services, configurations, and dependencies for easy deployment across environments.

When publishing your anihpj Django application, this command bundles Django web services, database configurations, worker processes, and supporting infrastructure into distributable packages that can be deployed to different environments while maintaining service relationships and configuration consistency.

Syntax

# Publish compose application
docker compose publish [REPOSITORY]

# Publish with specific tag
docker compose publish myregistry/anihpj:v1.0.0

# Publish with custom configuration
docker compose publish --platform linux/amd64 myregistry/anihpj:latest

Parameters Description

  • REPOSITORY: Target registry repository for Django application publication. Use format 'registry/namespace/app:tag' for versioned Django deployment packages.
  • --platform: Target platform for published application. Specify architecture for Django deployment across different cloud providers and environments.
  • --tag: Additional tags for published application. Enable Django application versioning and environment-specific package management.

Attributes

  • Package Size: Total size of published Django application bundle including all services and dependencies.
  • Service Count: Number of Django services included in published package. Track application complexity and deployment scope.
  • Configuration Bundle: Compose files, environment variables, and service definitions packaged with Django application.
  • Version Metadata: Tags, labels, and version information attached to published Django application package.
  • Distribution Status: Publication progress and registry upload status for Django application deployment packages.

Methods

Versioned Publication

Syntax: docker compose publish [REGISTRY]/[NAMESPACE]/[APP]:[TAG]

Parameters: REGISTRY, NAMESPACE, APP, TAG - Components of Django application package identifier

Publish Django application with specific version tags and registry targeting for controlled deployment and environment management.

Multi-Platform Publication

Syntax: docker compose publish --platform [PLATFORM] [REPOSITORY]

Parameters: PLATFORM - Target architecture, REPOSITORY - Publication destination

Publish Django applications for specific platforms and architectures, enabling deployment across diverse cloud and on-premises environments.

Return Value

Returns publication status including package size, upload progress, and registry confirmation for Django application distribution.

  • Success (0): Django application published successfully
  • Error (1): Publication failure due to registry access or package issues
  • Error (125): Invalid repository specification or configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Production Django Stack Publication (Enterprise Distribution)

Publish complete Django application stack to enterprise registry for standardized deployment across multiple production environments.

# Build and prepare Django application for publication
docker compose build --no-cache

# Publish to enterprise registry
docker compose publish enterprise-registry.company.com/anihpj/django-stack:v2.1.0

# Verify publication
docker search enterprise-registry.company.com/anihpj/django-stack

Django Models Context & Publication Strategy:

# Django models included in published stack
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    # Production-ready user management
    
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    author = models.ForeignKey(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    # Enterprise job posting functionality
    
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    # Skills verification system
    
# Publication package configuration
publication_config = {
    'package_name': 'enterprise-registry.company.com/anihpj/django-stack:v2.1.0',
    'included_services': [
        'web', 'worker', 'db', 'redis', 'nginx'
    ],
    'models_included': ['User', 'JobPost', 'Skills', 'Author', 'Location'],
    'environment_configs': [
        'production', 'staging', 'disaster_recovery'
    ],
    'features': [
        'authentication', 'job_management', 'skills_verification',
        'location_services', 'author_profiles'
    ],
    'deployment_targets': [
        'aws_ecs', 'kubernetes', 'docker_swarm'
    ]
}

# Publication metrics
print("Django Stack Publication Summary:")
print(f"Package: {publication_config['package_name']}")
print(f"Services: {len(publication_config['included_services'])}")
print(f"Models: {len(publication_config['models_included'])}")
print(f"Features: {len(publication_config['features'])}")
print(f"Deployment Targets: {len(publication_config['deployment_targets'])}")
Building Django application stack... ✅ Web service: anihpj_web:latest ✅ Worker service: anihpj_worker:latest ✅ Database: postgres:14 with migrations ✅ Cache: redis:6-alpine ✅ Proxy: nginx:alpine with SSL Publishing to enterprise-registry.company.com... 📦 Package: anihpj/django-stack:v2.1.0 📊 Total Size: 2.4GB 📋 Services: 5 components 🏷️ Tags: v2.1.0, latest, production-ready ✅ Publication Complete Registry: enterprise-registry.company.com/anihpj/django-stack:v2.1.0 Digest: sha256:a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8... Status: Available for deployment
>>>
